results.translator_notes:
1. 'Cadañales' (footnoted as 'cadañales o cadañeras' in the original) refers to annual plants — literally 'year-plants' — that complete their life cycle in one year. Translated as 'annual herbs' for clarity.
2. 'Higuerilla de infierno' is a traditional Spanish name for Ricinus communis (castor plant); the 'de infierno' qualifier ('of hell') alludes to its toxicity.
3. 'Calepino' refers to Ambrogio Calepino's famous polyglot dictionary (first published 1502), widely used in the 16th–17th centuries; Cobo cites it as authority for the vernacular term 'mata.'
4. The OCR rendered 'suyo' as 'ímyo' and 'doméstico' as 'dom^tke' and several other corruptions; all silently corrected where the intended word was unambiguous.
5. 'Mar del Sur' (South Sea) refers to the Pacific Ocean, the standard 17th-century Spanish term.
6. No medical applications are described in this segment; it is primarily botanical and economic in content.

results.title_inferred: The Avacollay and Other Columnar Cacti
results.original_text:
CAPITULO VI
Del avacollay y otros cardones

El cardón llamado avacollay es el mayor de los de esta segunda clase, en que se ponen todos los demás que son verdes, espinosos, acanalados y que no dan madera de provecho; y deben de pasar de diez o doce suertes dellos. El avacollay crece unas veces levantado y derecho, y otras, tendido por el suelo, que no parece sino culebra verde. Tiene de cuatro a seis esquinas; en la hechura se parece a un cirio de muchos pábulos. Es tan grueso como el brazo y largo desde uno hasta dos estados. Echa en su cumbre una flor blanca sin olor alguno, dos o tres veces mayor que una azucena, la cual comen los indios cocida. Son muchas las diferencias de cardones que se hallan entre el avacollay y el menor de todos, que es aquel linaje de cardoncillos poco más gruesos que el dedo pulgar, de que ya queda dicho; éstos llevan unas pitahayas del tamaño de madroños, cuyo zumo he visto dar a los héticos y dicen ser provechoso para esta enfermedad.

De las castas de cardones deste capítulo y clase unos son frutíferos y otros, no. Hállase cierta especie déllos más delgados y con menores y mayor número de esquinas que los primeros, de los cuales nacen tantos sobre un mismo pie y tronco, que viene a hacerse un árbol muy copado. Las diferencias de flores que producen estos cardones son muchas, que todas convienen en carecer de olor. Es muy digno de reparar en estos cardones, y que yo lo he ponderado no pocas veces, que nacen de ordinario entre piedras y riscos y no pocas veces sobre las mismas peñas; y lo que más acrecienta la admiración es que también suelen nacer en partes donde nunca llueve, como en estos Llanos del Perú; de suerte que sin tener jamás riego del cielo ni de la tierra, y estando en suelo seco de cascajo y peñas, sin rastro de humedad, estén ellos tan verdes y aguanosos como un pepino, pues, en dándoles una cuchillada, sale dellos mucha agua.
results.translation:
CHAPTER VI
On the Avacollay and Other Cardones [columnar cacti]

The cardón [columnar cactus] called avacollay is the largest of those in this second class, which comprises all the rest that are green, spiny, ribbed, and yield no useful timber; and there must be more than ten or twelve varieties of them. The avacollay grows sometimes upright and erect, and at other times sprawling along the ground, where it looks for all the world like a green serpent. It has four to six ribs [lit. corners]; in its form it resembles a candle with many wicks. It is as thick as an arm and between one and two fathoms [estados] in length. At its crown it bears a white flower entirely without scent, two or three times larger than a lily, which the Indians eat cooked. There are many varieties of cardones lying between the avacollay and the smallest of all—that kind of small cardoncillo [diminutive cactus] little thicker than a thumb, already described above—these last bear pitahayas [cactus fruits] the size of arbutus berries [madroños], whose juice I have seen administered to consumptives [héticos], and it is said to be beneficial for that disease.

Among the varieties of cardones in this chapter and class, some are fruit-bearing and others are not. There is found a certain species of them, more slender and with smaller but more numerous ribs than the first, from which so many stems arise from a single base and trunk that the whole forms a very densely branched tree. The varieties of flowers that these cardones produce are many, all of them alike in having no scent. It is very worthy of note regarding these cardones—and I have marveled at it not a few times—that they ordinarily grow among stones and crags, and not infrequently upon bare rock faces; and what increases the wonder still more is that they also tend to grow in places where it never rains, as in these Llanos [coastal plains] of Peru; so that without ever receiving moisture from sky or earth, and standing in dry ground of gravel and bare rock with no trace of humidity, they are nonetheless as green and water-laden as a cucumber—for when one cuts into them, much water flows out.

entities_mentioned.name: avacollay
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Probably Browningia candelaris or a large Andean columnar cactus (Cereus or Echinopsis/Trichocereus spp.); the name is Quechua
entities_mentioned.notes: Described as having 4–6 ribs, arm-thickness, 1–2 estados tall, with a large white odorless flower eaten cooked by Indigenous people. The sprawling growth habit may indicate more than one taxon is subsumed under this name.

entities_mentioned.name: cardoncillo (small cactus with pitahayas)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Possibly a small Peruvian columnar cactus such as Corryocactus brevistylus or related species bearing edible fruits
entities_mentioned.notes: Stems described as only slightly thicker than a thumb; fruits (pitahayas) the size of arbutus berries (madroños). Juice used medicinally for héticos (consumptives/those with wasting disease, likely pulmonary tuberculosis or similar).

entities_mentioned.name: pitahaya
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Generic Andean/Latin American term for cactus fruit; here probably a small species, not the large Hylocereus pitahaya of tropical regions
entities_mentioned.notes: Described as the size of madroños (Arbutus unedo berries, roughly 1–2 cm diameter).

results.translator_notes: 1. 'Héticos' (lit. 'hectics') refers to those suffering from hectic fever, classically associated with consumption/pulmonary tuberculosis or other chronic wasting conditions; rendered as 'consumptives' for clarity. 2. 'Estado' is a Spanish unit of height approximately equal to a fathom or the height of a man (~1.7 m), so the plant ranges roughly 1.7–3.4 m. 3. 'Cirio de muchos pábulos' — a wax candle with multiple wicks — is Cobo's vivid simile for the multi-ribbed columnar form; 'pábulos' is an archaic/OCR variant of 'pabilos' (wicks), corrected silently. 4. The observation that these cacti thrive in the absolutely rainless coastal Peruvian desert (Llanos) and contain abundant internal water is botanically accurate and represents a genuine natural-historical insight on Cobo's part. 5. Chapter cross-references ('ya queda dicho') indicate a preceding chapter described the smallest cardoncillos; this is noted for archival cross-linking.

results.title_inferred: On the Achuma cactus and the Cardón of Erysipelas
results.original_text:
CAPITULO VII
De la achuma

La achuma es cierta especie de cardón de los del segundo género; crece un estado de alto y a veces más; es tan grueso como la pierna, cuadrado y de color de zabila; produce unas pitahayas pequeñas y dulces. Es ésta una planta con que el demonio tenía engañados a los indios del Perú en su gentilidad; de la cual usaban para sus embustes y supersticiones. Bebido el zumo della, saca de sentido de manera que quedan los que lo beben como muertos, y aun se ha visto morir algunos por causa de la mucha frialdad que el cerebro recibe. Transportados con esta bebida los indios, soñaban mil disparates y los creían como si fueran verdades. Es de temperamento frío en el tercero grado y húmedo en el segundo; aprovecha su zumo contra las intemperies cálidas, contra el ardor de los riñones; y bebido en poca cantidad, es bueno contra las calenturas largas, contra la itiricia y ardor de orina.

CAPITULO VIII
Del cardón de la isípula

A esta especie de cardón le damos nombre del efecto que hace, mas el vulgo lo llama muérete por mí. Es muy medicinal y a esta causa lo suelen algunas personas criar en sus casas; crece de uno a dos estados en alto; el tronco es grueso como el brazo, del cual van saliendo en lugar de ramas muchos trozos, y déstos, unos se van encaramando en otros; y todos estos trozos o ramos están armados de unas espinillas del tamaño de alfileres pequeños, y junto a las espinas echa en lugar de hojas infinitos picos o pezoncillos muy semejantes en el tamaño y forma a las vainillas verdes en que los rábanos producen la semilla, sólo que son más tiernos y aguanosos. Con esta planta se cura la isípula y el calor del hígado, mojada y aplicada sobre la parte en que está el mal.
results.translation:
CHAPTER VII
On the Achuma

The achuma is a certain species of cardón [columnar cactus] of the second genus; it grows to a height of one estado [approximately six feet] and sometimes more; it is as thick as a leg, square in cross-section, and of the color of aloe [zabila]; it produces small, sweet pitahayas [cactus fruits]. This is a plant with which the Devil had deceived the Indians of Peru in their paganism, and which they used for their deceptions and superstitions. When its juice is drunk, it deprives one of one's senses in such a way that those who drink it remain as if dead; indeed, some have been seen to die from it, owing to the great chill that the brain receives. Transported by this drink, the Indians would dream a thousand absurdities and believed them as if they were truths. [Footnote: From the name of this plant there doubtless derives the present-day Creole verb of Peru and Chile chumarse, a synonym for getting drunk or intoxicated.] It is of a cold temperament in the third degree and moist in the second; its juice is beneficial against hot intemperances [excessive heat conditions], against burning of the kidneys; and drunk in small quantity, it is good against prolonged fevers, against jaundice [itiricia], and burning of urine.

CHAPTER VIII
On the Cardón of Erysipelas

We give this species of cardón its name from the effect it produces, though the common people call it muérete por mí ["die for me"]. It is highly medicinal, and for this reason some people are accustomed to grow it in their homes. It grows from one to two estados in height; the trunk is as thick as an arm, from which, in place of branches, many segments emerge, some of which climb upon others; and all these segments or branches are armed with small spines the size of little pins. Beside the spines, in place of leaves, it puts forth countless points or small nipples very similar in size and shape to the small green pods in which radishes produce their seed, except that they are more tender and watery. With this plant, erysipelas [isípula] and heat of the liver are treated, [the plant being] moistened and applied over the affected part.

entities_mentioned.name: achuma
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Echinopsis pachanoi (San Pedro cactus) or possibly Trichocereus peruvianus
entities_mentioned.notes: Described as columnar, roughly four-sided, leg-thick, aloe-colored, producing small sweet pitahayas. Used ritually as a hallucinogen by Peruvian indigenous peoples. Cobo describes it as cold in the third degree and moist in the second [Galenic humoral classification]. Medical uses: juice used against hot conditions, kidney inflammation, prolonged fevers, jaundice, and painful urination. Preparation: juice drunk, quantity modulated (small doses for fevers/jaundice/dysuria). The footnote notes the derived Creole verb chumarse (to get drunk).

entities_mentioned.name: cardón de la isípula / muérete por mí
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Possibly a species of Euphorbia or a small Opuntia / Rhipsalis relative; identification uncertain
entities_mentioned.notes: Described as a branching cactus-like plant growing 1–2 estados tall, trunk arm-thick, with pin-sized spines and small fleshy nipple-like projections resembling green radish seed pods. Kept in homes for its medicinal value. Condition treated: erysipelas (isípula) and hepatic heat [calor del hígado]. Preparation: plant moistened and applied topically to the affected area (poultice).

entities_mentioned.name: pitahayas
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Hylocereus spp. or Stenocereus spp. fruit (dragon fruit genus)
entities_mentioned.notes: Used here descriptively to characterize the fruit of the achuma as small and sweet.

entities_mentioned.name: zabila
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Aloe vera
entities_mentioned.notes: Referenced only for color comparison to the achuma cactus.

results.translator_notes: 1. 'Estado' is a period Spanish unit of length approximately equal to the height of a man (~1.7–1.8 m / ~6 ft). 2. 'Itiricia' is a period spelling of 'ictericia' (jaundice); rendered as jaundice in the translation. 3. 'Isípula' is a period Spanish rendering of erysipelas (a streptococcal skin infection), also used loosely for other inflammatory skin conditions. 4. 'Temperamento frío en el tercero grado y húmedo en el segundo' reflects standard Galenic humoral pharmacology of the era. 5. The footnote in the original (marked as note 5) is editorial, added by a later editor of the Cobo text, not by Cobo himself; it has been incorporated in the translation in brackets. 6. OCR artifact 'lodos' corrected to 'todos'; 'cap¬dón' and other hyphenation artifacts silently resolved. 7. The identification of achuma as San Pedro cactus (Echinopsis pachanoi) is well-supported in ethnobotanical literature; Cobo's description of its psychoactive properties and ritual use is among the earliest colonial accounts of mescaline-containing cactus use.

results.title_inferred: Medicinal Properties and Uses of Coca Leaf (Part 2)
results.original_text:
es la que, después de seca, queda con su color verde, tiesa y lisa. Finalmente, es la yerba más delicada que se puede imaginar, porque le daña aire, sol, agua y humedad.

Su temperamento es caliente y seco, con muy buena estipticidad: mascada de ordinario, aparta de los dientes toda corrupción y neguijón, y los emblanquece, aprieta y conforta. A mí me sucedió que llamando una vez a un barbero para que me sacara una muela, porque se andaba y me dolía mucho, me dijo el barbero que era lástima sacarla, porque estaba buena y sana; y como se hallase presente un amigo mío religioso, me aconsejó que mascase coca por algunos días. Hícelo así, con que se me quitó el dolor de la muela y ella se afijó como las demás. El zumo de la coca conforta el estómago y ayuda a la digestión; quita toda la ventosidad y mal de ijada. Los polvos desta hoja, tomados de ordinario y que a dos partes dellos se eche una de azúcar, son contra el asma o ronquera del pecho. La semilla de la coca tomada en sahumerio, dicen los indios que estanca todo flujo de sangre de narices; y el cocimiento della, bebido con miel de abejas y yerba buena, aprovecha a la relajación del estómago y contra los vómitos. El cocimiento de la hoja bebido de ordinario, vale contra las cámaras, deseca las llagas y las mundifica; los polvos, mezclados con sal y clara de huevo, consolidan y aprietan toda fractura y disolación de huesos; y echados en poca cantidad en las úlceras, las desecan y encoran; y el mismo efecto hacen en las llagas de los disciplinantes, como el polvo del arrayán. Finalmente, entra la coca, por su estipticidad, en los vinos y cocimientos estípticos, y hace su confortación como los demás constipantes y confortantes.
results.translation:
it is that which, after drying, retains its green color, stiff and smooth. In sum, it is the most delicate herb imaginable, for it is damaged by air, sun, water, and moisture.

Its temperament [complexion] is hot and dry, with a very pronounced astringency [estipticidad]: chewed regularly, it removes all corruption and decay [neguijón, i.e., dental caries/tartar] from the teeth, and whitens, tightens, and strengthens them. It happened to me that, having once called for a barber-surgeon to extract a molar because it was loose and causing me great pain, the barber told me it would be a pity to remove it, as it was sound and healthy; and as a religious friend of mine happened to be present, he advised me to chew coca for some days. I did so, and my toothache went away and the tooth became as firm as the rest. The juice of coca fortifies the stomach and aids digestion; it dispels all flatulence [ventosidad] and pain in the side [mal de ijada, i.e., flank or kidney pain/colic]. The powders of this leaf, taken regularly with one part sugar to every two parts powder, are effective against asthma or hoarseness of the chest. The seed of the coca, taken as a fumigation [sahumerio], the Indians say arrests all nosebleeds [flujo de sangre de narices]; and a decoction of it, drunk with honey and spearmint [yerba buena], is beneficial for weakness of the stomach and against vomiting. A decoction of the leaf, drunk regularly, is effective against diarrhea [cámaras], dries out sores and cleanses them; the powders, mixed with salt and egg white [clara de huevo], consolidate and bind all fractures and dislocations [disolación] of bones; and applied in small quantities to ulcers, they dry and heal them; and they produce the same effect on the wounds of penitents [llagas de los disciplinantes], as does the powder of myrtle [arrayán]. Finally, coca enters, by virtue of its astringency, into astringent wines and decoctions, and exerts its strengthening effect in the same manner as other binding and fortifying agents.

entities_mentioned.name: coca
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Erythroxylum coca
entities_mentioned.notes: The primary subject of the passage. Cobo describes it as hot and dry in temperament with strong astringent properties. Multiple preparations described: chewing the leaf, expressed juice, leaf powder, seed fumigation, and leaf decoction.

entities_mentioned.name: neguijón
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: dental caries or tartar/tooth decay
entities_mentioned.notes: Period Spanish term for rotting or decay of the teeth; here treated by chewing coca leaf.

entities_mentioned.name: mal de ijada
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: flank pain, possibly renal colic or intestinal colic
entities_mentioned.notes: Literally 'pain of the flank/side'; a common humoral-era complaint encompassing kidney, liver, or intestinal pain.

entities_mentioned.name: yerba buena
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Mentha spicata (spearmint) or Mentha spp.
entities_mentioned.notes: Used here in combination with honey and a coca decoction against stomach weakness and vomiting.

entities_mentioned.name: arrayán
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Myrtus communis (myrtle) or a related New World Myrtaceae species
entities_mentioned.notes: Powder of arrayán cited as a comparator for healing the wounds of flagellant penitents [disciplinantes]. In colonial Peru, local Myrtaceae species were often called arrayán.

entities_mentioned.name: miel de abejas
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: honey (Apis mellifera)
entities_mentioned.notes: Combined with coca decoction and spearmint for stomach relaxation and vomiting.

results.translator_notes: Several OCR errors have been silently corrected: 'naricea' → 'narices' (nosebleeds); 'eonso- lidan' → 'consolidan'; 'dara de huevo' → 'clara de huevo' (egg white); 'disolación' is retained as a period spelling variant of 'dislocación/disolución' meaning dislocation or separation of bones; 'encoran' is an archaic verb meaning to form a scab/heal over (modern: 'encostrar' or 'cicatrizar'). The anecdote about Cobo's own toothache is a notable first-person autobiographical passage. 'Llagas de los disciplinantes' refers to the self-inflicted wounds of religious flagellant penitents, a common Counter-Reformation practice. The schema of 'two parts powder to one part sugar' for the asthma remedy is preserved from the original. 'Vinos y cocimientos estípticos' refers to astringent medicinal wines and decoctions, a standard category in Galenic pharmacy.

results.title_inferred: Palta (Avocado): Culinary Uses, Medical Properties, and Nomenclature
results.original_text: mas se parecen a las brevas; unas son redondas y otras prolongadas, y tienen la cáscara tan sutil y delicada como la de las ciruelas. En algunas partes preparan la palta verde hecha pequeñas tajadas y echadas en salmuera, para suplir la falta de aceitunas. El hueso de la palta, dado a beber en polvos con agua de llantén o almacigada y acerada, estanca las cámaras. El aceite que dél se saca es bueno para curar los empeines; y si con el zumo deste hueso se tiñe algún lienzo y se oscurece en él alguna señal, no se quita jamás el color que toma, que es rojo. La madera del árbol sirve para fábricas y para leña, que para entrambas cosas es buena. La palta se llama así en la lengua general del Perú, que en la mayor parte de las Indias la nombran aguacate, que es el nombre que le dan los indios de la isla Española.
results.translation: they resemble figs [brevas]; some are round and others elongated, and they have a skin as fine and delicate as that of plums. In some places, the unripe palta is prepared in small slices and placed in brine, to substitute for the lack of olives. The pit [hueso] of the palta, given as a drink in powdered form with plantain water [agua de llantén], or with mastic water [almacigada] and iron-steeped water [acerada], stanches looseness of the bowels [cámaras]. The oil extracted from it is good for treating ringworm [empeines]; and if a cloth is stained with the juice of this pit and a mark appears upon it, the color it takes—which is red—can never be removed. The wood of the tree serves for building and for firewood, being good for both purposes. The palta is called by that name in the general language of Peru [Quechua], whereas in most of the Indies it is called aguacate, which is the name given to it by the Indians of Hispaniola [isla Española].

entities_mentioned.name: palta / aguacate
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Persea americana (avocado)
entities_mentioned.notes: Cobo explicitly equates 'palta' (Quechua term, still used in Peru and Chile) with 'aguacate' (Nahuatl-derived term used across most of Spanish America). Both names remain in use today.

entities_mentioned.name: agua de llantén
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Plantago major or related Plantago spp. (common plantain, not the banana)
entities_mentioned.notes: Plantain water was a standard colonial-era preparation used as an astringent; here combined with the powdered avocado pit to treat diarrhea [cámaras].

entities_mentioned.name: agua almacigada
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Water infused with mastic resin (Pistacia lentiscus)
entities_mentioned.notes: Mastic-steeped water was a common astringent/anti-diarrheal preparation in Galenic and early modern Iberian medicine.

entities_mentioned.name: agua acerada
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Iron-steeped water (chalybeate water)
entities_mentioned.notes: 'Acerada' refers to water in which iron (acero = steel) has been steeped, a standard preparation for astringent and tonic purposes in early modern medicine.

entities_mentioned.name: empeines
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Ringworm / dermatophytosis or other skin eruptions
entities_mentioned.notes: 'Empeines' in colonial Spanish medical usage could refer broadly to skin eruptions, including ringworm, eczema-like conditions, or herpes. Avocado oil is applied topically.

entities_mentioned.name: cámaras
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Diarrhea / dysentery (looseness of the bowels)
entities_mentioned.notes: Standard early modern Spanish medical term for frequent loose stools or dysentery.

results.translator_notes: The segment is a continuation (fragment beginning mid-sentence) of a longer description of the palta/avocado fruit, likely preceded by a comparison of its shape and skin. The OCR errors corrected include: 'desti hueso' → 'deste hueso'; 'paltn' → 'palta'; 'sirv-e' → 'sirve'; 'XL oscurece' → 'y se oscurece' (the 'XL' appears to be a page-break artifact or OCR misread of 'y se'). The dyeing property of the avocado pit juice (producing a permanent reddish-brown stain on cloth) is a well-attested phenomenon and aligns with the use of avocado pit tannins as a natural dye. Cobo's note on nomenclature is ethnolinguistically significant: he correctly distinguishes the Quechua term 'palta' from the Nahuatl-derived 'aguacate,' and erroneously (or loosely) attributes the latter to the 'Indians of Hispaniola' rather than Mesoamerica—a minor geographical imprecision common in 17th-century sources.

results.title_inferred: The Quina-Quina Tree: Properties, Preparations, and Medical Uses
results.original_text:
CAPITULO LXXXI
De la quina-quina

Llaman en el Perú a un árbol grande y hermoso como un melo o olivo; la hoja, del tamaño y talle que la del limón ceutí; el tronco es algo colorado, resinoso y aromático. Es árbol caliente en el segundo grado, estíptico y seco y de suave olor. Echa unas pepitas por semilla del tamaño de almendras, de color amarillo y de sustancia oleaginosa, que con fragancia huelen amigablemente; son asimismo calientes y estípticas en el segundo grado, y secas en más del primero. Sajando el tronco y ramas destila una resina olorosa, que se congela tanto, que se muele en polvos y queda de color negro claro, la cual es caliente y más seca que las pepitas. Nace este árbol en la tierra caliente de la provincia de los Charcas, en el Perú. Si con su corteza se limpia de ordinario la dentadura, la aprieta y conforta; y el palo, raspado y cocido con polipodio, hojas de sen y anís, y el cocimiento tomado en ayunas algunas mañanas, desopila el estómago, hígado y bazo, mundifica y limpia la vejiga. Las hojas, majadas y puestas sobre las heridas frescas, las desecan y juntan, y el cocimiento dellas con salmuera, hojas de chilca y molle deshincha las piernas gotosas. De las pepitas de este árbol se hace un aceite maravilloso para toda herida fresca, el cual se usa mucho en Potosí y hácese desta manera: majadas cuatro onzas déstas pepitas, se echan en la cuarta parte de un cuartillo de vino añejo por espacio de dos horas, y luego se echa todo en dos libras y media de aceite, y a fuego manso cuece hasta que se consume el vino; y quitado del fuego y frío, se cuela y se vuelve a la olla o cazo, y se añade una libra de trementina común, y con ella da un hervor no más; y apartado del fuego, se le echan de polvos de incienso y de mirra, de cada cosa onza y media, sutilmente molidos, y se menean para que se incorporen; y tapado el vaso, se guarda; y hace maravillosos efectos.

Demás desto, sahumándose así con las pepitas como con la resina, se quitan los dolores de cabeza. Las pepitas tostadas y tomadas con vino son contra el dolor de ijada y ventosidades, y majadas, mezcladas con polvos de la resina y todo ello cocido con vino con un poco de la resina de molle, incienso y miel de abejas, aprovecha el cocimiento, después de colado, contra las llagas sucias y cavernosas, porque mundifica y deseca con suavidad. La resina, sutilmente molida y hervida con aceite común o con tocino o manteca, junta las heridas frescas; y el polvo, echado sobre la herida, atrae cuanta humedad tiene y la deseca.
results.translation:
CHAPTER LXXXI
On the Quina-Quina

In Peru they give this name to a large and handsome tree resembling a quince or olive; the leaf is the size and shape of that of the Ceuti lemon [a variety of citron]; the trunk is somewhat reddish, resinous, and aromatic. The tree is hot in the second degree [of the Galenic humoral scale], astringent [estíptico], dry, and of a pleasant odor. It produces seeds the size of almonds, yellow in color and of an oily substance, which smell agreeably with a fine fragrance; these too are hot and astringent in the second degree, and dry in more than the first. When the trunk and branches are scored, they exude a fragrant resin which congeals so thoroughly that it can be ground into powder, at which point it is of a clear black color; this resin is hot and drier than the seeds. This tree grows in the hot lowlands of the province of Charcas, in Peru.

If the teeth are regularly cleaned with the bark, it tightens and strengthens them. The wood, scraped and boiled together with polypody [Polypodium], senna leaves, and anise, and the decoction taken on an empty stomach for several mornings, clears obstructions of the stomach, liver, and spleen, and purifies and cleanses the bladder. The leaves, pounded and applied to fresh wounds, dry and close them; and a decoction of the leaves with brine, leaves of chilca, and molle reduces swelling in gouty legs.

From the seeds of this tree a marvelous oil is made for all fresh wounds, which is much used in Potosí, and it is prepared in the following manner: four ounces of these seeds are pounded and steeped in a quarter of a quartillo [a small liquid measure] of aged wine for the space of two hours; the whole is then added to two and a half pounds of oil and cooked over a low flame until the wine is consumed; removed from the fire and allowed to cool, it is strained and returned to the pot or pan, and one pound of common turpentine is added, with which it is brought to a single boil; taken from the fire, one and a half ounces each of finely ground powders of frankincense and myrrh are stirred in so that they incorporate; the vessel is then stoppered and set aside, and it produces marvelous effects.

Beyond this, fumigating with the seeds as well as with the resin relieves headaches. The toasted seeds taken with wine are effective against flank pain [dolor de ijada] and flatulence; and pounded, mixed with powder of the resin, and all boiled in wine with a little resin of molle, frankincense, and honey, the decoction, once strained, is beneficial against foul and cavernous [deep, undermined] ulcers [llagas], because it cleanses and dries them gently. The resin, finely ground and boiled in common oil or in lard or fat, closes fresh wounds; and the powder, sprinkled upon a wound, draws out whatever moisture it holds and dries it.

entities_mentioned.name: quina-quina
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Myroxylon peruiferum or Myroxylon balsamum var. pereirae (Peruvian balsam tree / quinaquina); possibly also applied to Myroxylon toluiferum. Some scholars also associate the name with Cinchona spp., but Cobo's description of resin, seeds, and uses fits Myroxylon more closely.
entities_mentioned.notes: The name 'quina-quina' was applied to several different trees in colonial Peru. Cobo's description of a resinous tree yielding oily almond-sized yellow seeds and a dark solidifying resin points strongly toward Myroxylon spp. (balsam trees) rather than Cinchona (true quina/quinquina for fever). The province of Charcas (present-day Bolivia) is consistent with Myroxylon distribution.

entities_mentioned.name: polipodio
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Polypodium vulgare (common polypody fern) or a related New World species
entities_mentioned.notes: A standard ingredient in European and colonial pharmacopeias for deobstruent preparations.

entities_mentioned.name: sen (hojas de sen)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Senna alexandrina (senna), leaves used as a purgative
entities_mentioned.notes: Hojas de sen is a well-attested colonial pharmacopoeial term for senna leaves.

entities_mentioned.name: anís
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Pimpinella anisum (anise) or possibly Foeniculum vulgare (fennel); both were used interchangeably in colonial contexts

entities_mentioned.name: chilca
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Baccharis latifolia or Baccharis salicifolia (a common Andean shrub in the Asteraceae family)
entities_mentioned.notes: Chilca was widely used in Andean folk medicine for anti-inflammatory and wound-healing applications.

entities_mentioned.name: molle
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Schinus molle (Peruvian pepper tree / molle)
entities_mentioned.notes: Molle is consistently identified as Schinus molle throughout Cobo's Historia and other colonial sources. Both the leaves and resin appear here.

entities_mentioned.name: trementina (trementina común)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Common turpentine (oleoresin from Pinus spp.)
entities_mentioned.notes: A standard ingredient in wound salves of the period.

entities_mentioned.name: incienso
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Boswellia sacra or related Boswellia spp. (frankincense)

entities_mentioned.name: mirra
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Commiphora myrrha (myrrh)

entities_mentioned.name: limón ceutí
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Citrus medica (citron), a variety then associated with Ceuta or North Africa
entities_mentioned.notes: Used here only as a morphological comparator for leaf size and shape.

medical_extracts.name: quina-quina bark
medical_extracts.preparation: Rub the bark directly on the teeth as a regular dental hygiene practice

medical_extracts.name: quina-quina wood decoction
medical_extracts.condition_treated: Obstruction of the stomach, liver, and spleen; bladder impurities
medical_extracts.preparation: Scrape the wood and boil with polypody, senna leaves, and anise; take the decoction on an empty stomach for several mornings

medical_extracts.name: quina-quina leaf poultice
medical_extracts.preparation: Pound the leaves and apply directly to fresh wounds

medical_extracts.name: quina-quina leaf decoction with brine, chilca, and molle
medical_extracts.preparation: Boil leaves with brine, chilca leaves, and molle; apply or bathe affected area

medical_extracts.name: quina-quina seed oil (compound)
medical_extracts.preparation: Pound 4 oz of seeds; steep in a quarter quartillo of aged wine for 2 hours; add to 2.5 lbs of oil; cook over low heat until wine evaporates; strain when cool; return to vessel; add 1 lb common turpentine and bring to one boil; remove from heat; stir in 1.5 oz each of finely powdered frankincense and myrrh; seal and store

medical_extracts.name: quina-quina seed/resin fumigation
medical_extracts.preparation: Fumigate (inhale smoke from) burning seeds or resin

medical_extracts.name: quina-quina toasted seeds with wine
medical_extracts.preparation: Toast seeds and take with wine

medical_extracts.name: quina-quina compound decoction in wine
medical_extracts.preparation: Pound seeds, mix with powdered resin, boil in wine with molle resin, frankincense, and honey; strain and apply

medical_extracts.name: quina-quina resin in oil or fat
medical_extracts.preparation: Finely grind resin and boil in common oil, lard, or fat; apply to wound

medical_extracts.name: quina-quina resin powder
medical_extracts.preparation: Sprinkle dry powdered resin directly onto wound

results.translator_notes: 1. The OCR produced several corrupted characters and word breaks (e.g., 'q ii i n a ^ q u i n a', 'irW', 'cpie', 'X)itas', 'bácese' rendered as 'hácese'). All have been silently corrected where the intended reading is unambiguous. 2. The identification of 'quina-quina' is the most significant editorial challenge: the name was polysemous in colonial Peru, applied to Cinchona (the antimalarial bark, later called 'quina' or 'quinquina' in Europe) and to various resinous balsam trees (Myroxylon spp.). Cobo's description here — resinous trunk, oily almond-sized seeds, dark solidifying resin, absence of any mention of fever treatment — argues strongly against Cinchona and for Myroxylon or a related taxon. 3. Humoural-Galenic degree terminology ('hot in the second degree,' 'dry in more than the first') has been translated literally and bracketed in the translation to preserve period medical vocabulary. 4. 'Dolor de ijada' (flank pain) most likely refers to pain interpreted as renal or hepatic colic in the period's nosology. 5. 'Llagas cavernosas' refers to deep, undermined, or sinus-forming ulcers, a standard surgical category of the era. 6. The recipe for the compound wound oil is one of the more detailed pharmaceutical preparations in this section and follows conventions found in contemporaneous Spanish pharmacopoeias.

results.title_inferred: On Espingo: A Medicinal Pod from the Andean Frontier
results.original_text:
CAPITULO XC

Del espingo

Los indios gentiles de las provincias de los Andes, en el Perú, suelen sacar a los pueblos de su frontera unas vainillas como algarrobas, de color leonado oscuro, cuya sustancia, cuajada, es como sangre de drago, aunque reluciente y tirante a negra, y de suave y profundo olor. Produce estas vainas un árbol que nace en aquella tierra, llamado espingo. Sacan los indios bárbaros estas vainas como cosa preciosa, para rescatar con ella cuchillos, tijeras y otras menudencias, que ellos precian mucho; lo cual alcanzan con facilidad de los españoles, por ser tenidas por muy medicinales estas vainas; porque, con sus polvos, tomados en ayunas, en agua o vino aguado, se curan las camaras de sangre, y tomados por el mismo orden o en alguno de los lamedores de rosas secas o de arrayán, son contra el flujo de sangre de vena rota en el pecho, y contra el que sale por la vía de la orina.
results.translation:
CHAPTER XC

On Espingo

The pagan Indians [indios gentiles] of the Andean provinces of Peru customarily bring out to the frontier settlements certain small pods resembling carob beans [algarrobas], dark tawny in color, whose congealed substance resembles dragon's blood [sangre de drago], though more lustrous and tending toward black, with a gentle and deep fragrance. These pods are produced by a tree native to that land, called espingo. The barbarous Indians bring these pods out as something precious, to barter for knives, scissors, and other small goods that they value greatly; this they obtain with ease from the Spaniards, since these pods are held to be highly medicinal. For when their powder is taken on an empty stomach in water or diluted wine, it cures bloody fluxes [camaras de sangre; i.e., bloody dysentery]; and taken in the same manner, or in one of the electuaries [lamedores] of dried roses or of myrtle [arrayán], it is effective against hemorrhage from a broken vessel in the chest, and against blood passed through the urinary tract.

entities_mentioned.name: espingo
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Nectandra sp. or Ocotea sp. (Lauraceae); sometimes identified as Talauma ovata or Quararibea sp.; the precise identity remains debated. Many scholars associate 'espingo' with Ocotea quixos or a related Lauraceous aromatic pod.
entities_mentioned.notes: Espingo is a historically contested identification. The fragrant, dark pod described here is consistent with aromatic Lauraceae from the Andean piedmont. Some sources identify it with Quararibea funebris or Ocotea quixos; no definitive consensus exists.

entities_mentioned.name: sangre de drago
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Dragon's blood resin, likely from Croton lechleri (Euphorbiaceae) in the Peruvian context
entities_mentioned.notes: Used here as a color/texture comparator for the congealed interior substance of the espingo pod, not as a separate ingredient.

entities_mentioned.name: arrayán
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Myrtle, Myrtus communis or a New World congener (Myrtaceae)
entities_mentioned.notes: Used in compound electuary preparations; myrtle was a standard astringent in period pharmacy.

entities_mentioned.name: lamedores de rosas secas
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Electuary of dried roses; a standard pharmaceutical preparation of the period
entities_mentioned.notes: A lameor/lamedera (electuary, from Latin linctus) was a thick, lickable medicinal preparation. Dried rose electuaries were common European pharmacy items also used in colonial Spanish America.

results.title_inferred: The Guayroro Tree and Its Seeds
results.original_text:
CAPITULO XCI

Del guayroro

El guayroro es un árbol muy grande, que se halla en las provincias de los Andes, en el Perú. Echa unas vainas o canutos al modo de los de la amfistola, en cuya cavidad se hallan unas pepitas más redondas, aunque llanas y lisas, de color de un fino coral; tiene cada una, una mancha negra que la hermosea. Estiman mucho estas pepitas los indios, afirmando valer contra el mal de corazón y melancolías, tomando de sus polvos en vino o agua de azahar poco más de media drama [sic]. Demás de esto, dicen que traída una sarta de ellas al cuello que caiga sobre el pecho, aprovechan contra las tristezas de corazón, y que confortan la vista y cerebro.
results.translation:
CHAPTER XCI

On the Guayroro

The guayroro is a very large tree found in the provinces of the Andes, in Peru. It produces pods or tubes in the manner of those of the cassia fistula [amfistola], within whose cavity are found seeds that are rather round, though flat and smooth, of the color of fine coral; each one bears a black spot that lends it beauty. The Indians hold these seeds in great esteem, affirming that they are efficacious against ailments of the heart [mal de corazón] and melancholy, taken as a powder in wine or orange-blossom water [agua de azahar] at a dose of a little more than half a dram [drachm]. Beyond this, they say that when a string of them is worn around the neck so as to fall upon the chest, they are beneficial against sorrows of the heart [tristezas de corazón], and that they strengthen the sight and the brain.

entities_mentioned.name: guayroro
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Possibly Ormosia coccinea (scarlet Ormosia / huayruro) or a closely related Ormosia species (Fabaceae); the description of bright coral-red seeds with a black spot is a near-perfect match for huayruro seeds, which are still used in Andean amulets and jewelry
entities_mentioned.notes: The seeds of Ormosia coccinea and related species are iconic in Andean material culture; 'guayroro' is an early colonial spelling of the Quechua word 'huayruro'. The comparison to cassia fistula pods is apt in describing the legume pod form.

entities_mentioned.name: amfistola
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Cassia fistula (golden shower / cassia fistula, Fabaceae) — the senna-pod-bearing tree well known to early modern pharmacopoeias as 'cañafístola'
entities_mentioned.notes: Cobo uses the amfistola / cañafístola as a morphological reference point for the pod shape, not as a medicinal ingredient here.

entities_mentioned.name: agua de azahar
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Orange-blossom water, distilled from Citrus aurantium flowers
entities_mentioned.notes: A common pharmaceutical vehicle in 17th-century Iberian and colonial medicine, particularly associated with cardiac and nervine preparations.

results.translator_notes:
1. The source OCR renders 'los' as 'Im', 'unas' as 'nm', 'y' as 'i' (twice), 'la' as 'h', 'tristezas' as 'tritezás', and 'del' as 'd'; all silently corrected as unambiguous. 2. The bracketed '[sic]' appears in the original, apparently Cobo's own or a copyist's hesitation over the dosage figure; it has been retained and the unit glossed as 'drachm'. 3. The medical extraction follows below:

--- MATERIA MEDICA EXTRACTION ---
Name used: guayroro (seeds / pepitas)
Modern identification: Ormosia coccinea or related Ormosia spp. (huayruro)
Conditions treated: (a) mal de corazón (cardiac ailment / palpitations); (b) melancolías (melancholy / depression); (c) tristezas de corazón (grief, emotional sorrows); (d) weakness of sight; (e) weakness of the brain / mental faculties
Preparation & dose (internal): Powder of the seeds taken in wine or orange-blossom water, dose slightly above half a dram (~1.9 g)
Preparation (external / talismanic): Seeds strung into a necklace worn on the chest — described as a protective and comforting amulet; Cobo reports this usage without explicit endorsement or scepticism.

results.title_inferred: The Fever Tree and the Dysentery Tree: Two Medicinal Barks from the Diocese of Quito
results.original_text:
CAPITULO XCVII
Del árbol de calenturas

En los términos de la ciudad de Loja, diócesis de Quito, nace cierta casta de árboles grandes, que tienen la corteza como de canela, un poco más gruesa, y muy amarga; la cual, molida en polvos, se da a los que tienen calenturas y con sólo este remedio se quitan. Hánsede tomar estos polvos en cantidad del peso de dos reales en vino o en cualquiera otro licor poco antes que dé el frío. Son ya tan conocidos y estimados estos polvos, no sólo en todas las Indias, sino en Europa, con instancia los envían a pedir a Roma.

(Nota 13: Principalmente en los montes de Uritofinga, Loxa, Malacatos y Cajamarca. Las primeras especies que se descubrieron y usaron son la Cinchona macrocarpa y la C. pubescens.)

CAPITULO XCVIII
Del árbol que quita las cámaras

En la provincia de los Barbacoas, diócesis de Quito, que ha pocos años que la poblaron españoles, nace un género de árboles muy crecidos, cuya corteza es parda y casi tan gruesa como un dedo. Los polvos de esta corteza, bebidos en vino o en otro licor en cantidad de dos reales, quitan las cámaras. Ha poco tiempo que salió a luz esta planta y se conoció su virtud.
results.translation:
CHAPTER XCVII
Of the Fever Tree

Within the territory of the city of Loja, diocese of Quito, there grows a certain kind of large tree whose bark resembles that of cinnamon, though somewhat thicker and very bitter. This bark, ground into powder, is given to those suffering from fevers [calenturas], and by this remedy alone they are cured. The powder is to be taken in a quantity equal in weight to two reales [approximately 6–7 grams], dissolved in wine or any other liquid, shortly before the onset of the chill [el frío]. These powders are already so well known and highly esteemed, not only throughout the Indies but also in Europe, that they are urgently sent for as far as Rome.

(Footnote 13: Found principally in the mountains of Uritofinga, Loxa [Loja], Malacatos, and Cajamarca. The first species to be discovered and used are Cinchona macrocarpa and C. pubescens.)

CHAPTER XCVIII
Of the Tree That Cures Dysentery

In the province of the Barbacoas, diocese of Quito, which the Spanish have settled only a few years since, there grows a kind of very large tree whose bark is brown and nearly as thick as a finger. The powder of this bark, drunk in wine or another liquid in a quantity of two reales [in weight], cures dysentery [cámaras]. It is only recently that this plant came to light and its virtue became known.

entities_mentioned.name: Árbol de calenturas / polvos de corteza (Fever Tree / bark powder)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Cinchona spp. (Cinchona macrocarpa and Cinchona pubescens, per the footnote); source of quinine
entities_mentioned.notes: Explicitly identified in the footnote as Cinchona macrocarpa and C. pubescens. The city of Loja (present-day Ecuador) is historically associated with the first recorded use of cinchona bark for malaria. Dosage given as ~6–7g of powdered bark in wine or liquid, taken before the malarial chill. Condition treated: intermittent fevers [calenturas], consistent with malaria.

entities_mentioned.name: Árbol que quita las cámaras (Tree that cures dysentery)
entities_mentioned.notes: Located in the province of Barbacoas (modern Nariño, Colombia/Esmeraldas, Ecuador border region). Brown bark, thick as a finger, used in powdered form for dysentery [cámaras]. No species name given; the plant was apparently newly known at time of writing. Possibly a species of Croton, Quararibea, or another tannin-rich bark-bearing tree, but identification is speculative.

results.translator_notes: 1. The footnote (note 13) appears to contain OCR corruption: 'üritofinga [LViíozenca, nana o pico de loro, en qniehnal' has been interpreted as 'Uritofinga, Loxa [i.e., Loja]' with 'nana o pico de loro' likely being a Quechua or vernacular plant name reference, and 'qniehnal' likely 'quichua.' The cleaned text retains only the clearly legible place names and species names. 2. 'Dos reales' as a unit of weight (not currency) refers to the mass of two silver real coins, roughly 6–7 grams. 3. 'Cámaras' is a period Spanish term for dysentery or bloody flux (diarrhea with mucus/blood). 4. 'El frío' refers to the cold/shivering stage of a malarial paroxysm, confirming the fever-tree remedy targets malaria. 5. The reference to Rome (para enviar a Roma) likely alludes to the well-documented role of Jesuit intermediaries in distributing cinchona bark to Europe; this bark was famously known as 'Jesuit's bark' or 'Peruvian bark.'

results.title_inferred: The Tipa Tree: Description and Medical Uses
results.original_text:
CAPITULO CII

De la tipa

Los indios naturales de la provincia de los Charcas, en el Perú, llaman tipa a un árbol muy grande y hermoso, copado, de hojas verdes todo el año; algunos son tan crecidos como nogales. La hoja es del tamaño y hechura que la del lentisco, algún tanto mayor y blanquecina. Echa unas vainillas del largor de un dedo, delgadas y enjutas, salvo que junto al pezón son gruesecillas como si tuvieran dentro un garbanzo. Cada una de estas vainillas parece en la figura y talle a una ala de mariposa. El tronco es colorado y pesado, y así él como su corteza, ramas y hojas son muy estípticas y de complexión caliente; y principalmente la resina que destila el árbol, sajando su tronco y ramas, a la cual llaman sangre de tipa o sangre de drago del Perú, y es muy estimada por sus buenos efectos. Limpiando de ordinario los dientes con la corteza de este árbol, los conforta y encarna; y lo mismo hace si se enjuagan a menudo con el cocimiento de la corteza y resina; y si con este cocimiento echan ayudas, estanca todo género de cámaras, y lavando con él las almorranas estanca el flujo de sangre que suele haber en ellas. El cocimiento de sus hojas y corteza, bebido de ordinario, estanca el flujo de sangre así del pecho como del estómago y el que sale por la vía de la orina; y añadiendo al cocimiento bastante sal, y lavando con él las piernas hinchadas, las seca y deshincha, y conforta las de los gotosos; mayormente si para este efecto se añaden al dicho cocimiento hojas de molle, ramas de pinco-pinco y un poco de vino. Allende de lo dicho, el cocimiento de la resina con agua de cebada o de llantén con unas gotas de vinagre, un poco de alumbre y azúcar, lo que basta a endulzar el cocimiento, es maravilloso gargarismo para inflamaciones de la garganta; y principalmente cuando la campanilla está relajada o alargada. Demás de esto, habiendo llagas en la garganta, aprovecha el referido gargarismo con adición de miel de abejas, con que las mundifica y limpia. Finalmente, las hojas de este árbol, majadas y puestas sobre las heridas frescas, las unen con presteza.
results.translation:
CHAPTER CII

Of the Tipa

The native Indians of the province of Charcas, in Peru, call tipa a very large and beautiful tree, full-crowned, with green leaves throughout the year; some specimens grow as tall as walnut trees. The leaf is the size and shape of that of the mastic tree [lentisco], somewhat larger and whitish. It produces small pods about a finger's length, slender and dry, except near the stem where they are slightly thickened as if a chickpea were contained within. Each of these pods resembles in shape and form a butterfly's wing. The trunk is reddish and heavy, and both it and its bark, branches, and leaves are very astringent [estípticos] and of a hot complexion [complexión caliente]; most notably the resin that the tree exudes when its trunk and branches are incised, which they call sangre de tipa or sangre de drago of Peru [blood of the tipa, or dragon's blood of Peru], and it is highly esteemed for its beneficial effects. Cleaning the teeth regularly with the bark of this tree strengthens and restores [encarna] them; and the same effect is achieved if one rinses frequently with a decoction of the bark and resin. If enemas [ayudas] are administered with this decoction, it arrests all manner of fluxes of the bowels [cámaras]; and washing hemorrhoids [almorranas] with it stanches the bleeding that commonly accompanies them. The decoction of its leaves and bark, taken regularly as a drink, arrests bleeding from the chest as well as from the stomach, and that which passes through the urine. Adding a good quantity of salt to the decoction and washing swollen legs with it dries and reduces the swelling, and brings relief to those suffering from gout; especially if, for this purpose, leaves of molle, branches of pinco-pinco, and a little wine are added to the said decoction. Beyond the foregoing, a decoction of the resin made with barley water or plantain water, with a few drops of vinegar, a little alum, and enough sugar to sweeten the decoction, makes a marvelous gargle [gargarismo] for inflammations of the throat; most particularly when the uvula [campanilla] is relaxed or elongated. Furthermore, when there are sores in the throat, the aforementioned gargle with the addition of honey is beneficial, as it cleanses and purifies them [mundifica y limpia]. Finally, the leaves of this tree, pounded and applied to fresh wounds, close them quickly.

entities_mentioned.name: tipa
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Tipuana tipu (Benth.) Kuntze (family Fabaceae)
entities_mentioned.notes: A large leguminous tree native to the Andean valleys of Bolivia and northwestern Argentina, consistent with Cobo's description of the Charcas region. The winged pods described (resembling a butterfly wing) are characteristic samaras of T. tipu.

entities_mentioned.name: sangre de tipa / sangre de drago del Perú
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Resin of Tipuana tipu; the epithet 'sangre de drago' was applied broadly in Spanish colonial sources to various red resins
entities_mentioned.notes: Cobo equates this resin with 'sangre de drago' (dragon's blood), a name used in Iberian and colonial pharmacopoeias for several astringent red resins, most famously from Croton lechleri. Here it clearly refers to the resin of Tipuana tipu.

entities_mentioned.name: lentisco
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Pistacia lentiscus L. (mastic tree)
entities_mentioned.notes: Used by Cobo as a comparative reference for leaf size and shape, not as a subject of treatment.

entities_mentioned.name: molle
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Schinus molle L. (Peruvian pepper tree)
entities_mentioned.notes: Added to the decoction for treatment of swollen legs and gout.

entities_mentioned.name: pinco-pinco
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Ephedra sp. (likely Ephedra americana or related Andean species)
entities_mentioned.notes: Andean shrub used medicinally; branches added to decoction for swollen legs and gout.

entities_mentioned.name: alumbre
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Alum (potassium aluminium sulfate)
entities_mentioned.notes: Used as an astringent component of the gargle preparation.

entities_mentioned.name: agua de llantén
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Plantago major or P. lanceolata infusion
entities_mentioned.notes: Plantain water used as a vehicle for the gargle preparation.

results.title_inferred: The Capirona Tree and Its Petrifying Properties
results.original_text:
CAPITULO CXXVII
De la capirona

En la provincia del Paraguay y en otras tierras calientes de este reino del Perú nace cierto árbol, que en la gobernación de Yaguarsongo, diócesis de Quito, llaman capirona, el cual es de madera recia, y de tan particular naturaleza, que metido en el agua por tiempo, se convierte en piedra; y como nazca comúnmente en las orillas de los ríos, las ramas que caen dentro del agua, quedando parte fuera de ella, tienen de piedra aquella parte que cubrió el agua, y lo demás se queda palo como lo era antes; y es tan recia, dura y densa esta piedra, que sirve de pedernal y herida con el eslabón, despide muchas centellas.
results.translation:
CHAPTER CXXVII
Of the capirona

In the province of Paraguay and in other warm lands of this kingdom of Peru there grows a certain tree which, in the governorship of Yaguarsongo, diocese of Quito, is called capirona. It is of hard wood and of so particular a nature that, when submerged in water for a period of time, it turns to stone. And since it commonly grows on the banks of rivers, the branches that fall into the water—with part remaining above the surface—have turned to stone in that portion covered by the water, while the rest remains wood as it was before. This stone is so hard, dense, and firm that it serves as flint [pedernal], and when struck with a steel [eslabón], it emits many sparks.

entities_mentioned.name: capirona
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Calycophyllum spruceanum (Benth.) Hook.f. ex K.Schum. (family Rubiaceae), a hardwood tree native to Amazonian riparian zones, commonly called capirona or capirona de altura
entities_mentioned.notes: Cobo's account of petrification is likely a folk-naturalist observation of silicification or mineralization of wood submerged in mineral-rich river water, a real phenomenon (permineralization), rather than true lithification. The tree's extreme hardness is consistent with Calycophyllum spruceanum, one of the densest Amazonian hardwoods. No medical application is described in this segment; the chapter focuses on the tree's physical and mineralogical properties. The geographic reference to the gobernación of Yaguarsongo places it in the upper Amazon basin, present-day southeastern Ecuador/northern Peru.

entities_mentioned.name: Yaguarsongo
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Historical gobernación in the upper Marañón/Amazon region, roughly corresponding to present-day Zamora-Chinchipe province, Ecuador, and adjacent Peruvian territory
entities_mentioned.notes: A colonial administrative district within the Diocese of Quito.

results.translator_notes: No medical application or preparation method appears in this segment; it is a naturalistic/mineralogical description. The claim that wood submerged in water 'turns to stone' reflects a period understanding of permineralization or silicification. The detail that only the submerged portion petrifies while the exposed portion remains wood suggests direct empirical observation, however interpreted through a pre-scientific framework. 'Pedernal' (flint) and 'eslabón' (fire steel) are preserved in brackets in the translation as period-technical terms for fire-starting equipment.

results.title_inferred: Refutation of the Theory that Solomon's Fleets Populated the New World
results.original_text:
ara caminar y con que más fácilmente trajinasen desde las minas a los puertos los ricos metales que de tan lejanas tierras venían a buscar, ya que de las otras cosas no se curasen?

Ni es conforme a razón el intentar satisfacer a todas estas dificultades con decir que durante aquel comercio y navegación trujeron a esta tierra las mismas cosas que han traído los españoles, mas que, andando el tiempo, vinieron a extinguirse y acabarse. Porque las plantas y animales traídos por los nuestros, han cundido tanto por este Nuevo Mundo, que en muchas partes nacen tan copiosamente sin industria ni beneficio humano, que cuando en quererlas acabar y consumir pusieran los hombres toda su inteligencia y conato, no salieron con ello; cuanto más que no la ponen sino en conservarlas y aumentarlas, llevados del interés y utilidad que de las tales cosas les resulta. En conclusión, no vemos alguna diferencia de una región a otra en carecer de todos estos indicios, que es otro argumento bien fuerte; porque no hay duda sino que en las partes que aquellas flotas más frecuentaron con su contratación, hubieran quedado algunas señales y memorias que en las otras; porque no pudiera dejar de pegárseles algo a los naturales dellas de la comunicación y trato con los hebreos, y no pasa así, sino que todos son unos, así los de tierras ricas como los de poblaciones; y los marítimos como los que habitan la tierra adentro. Y la misma fuerza tienen estos argumentos contra los que presumen responder que cuando las flotas de Salomón navegaban a esta tierra, no estaba ella poblada de otras gentes, sino que entonces la comenzaron a poblar los hebreos, que si conceden que ya de antes estaba poblada de indios.
results.translation:
...for traveling, and so that they might more easily transport from the mines to the ports the rich metals which they came to seek from such distant lands—given that they took no care for anything else?

Nor is it reasonable to attempt to answer all these difficulties by saying that during that commerce and navigation they brought to this land the same things that the Spaniards have brought, but that, as time passed, these things came to be extinguished and to perish. For the plants and animals brought by our own people have spread so widely throughout this New World that in many places they grow so abundantly without human industry or cultivation, that even if men were to devote all their intelligence and effort to destroying and consuming them, they would not succeed; all the more so since they apply themselves not to destroying but to conserving and increasing them, driven by the profit and utility they derive from such things. In conclusion, we see no difference from one region to another in the absence of all these traces, which is another very strong argument; for there is no doubt that in those parts most frequently visited by those fleets in their trading, some signs and memories would have remained beyond those found elsewhere; for something could not have failed to rub off on the native peoples through communication and dealings with the Hebrews, and yet this is not the case—rather, all [the natives] are alike, whether those of wealthy lands or those of [smaller] settlements, and those of the coasts as well as those who dwell inland. And these same arguments carry equal force against those who would answer that when Solomon's fleets sailed to this land, it was not yet inhabited by other peoples, but that the Hebrews then began to settle it—[an argument that fails] if they grant that it was already inhabited by Indians beforehand.

entities_mentioned.name: Solomon's fleets [flotas de Salomón]
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Legendary fleet of King Solomon, referenced in 1 Kings 9–10; a common early modern hypothesis about pre-Columbian contact with the Americas
entities_mentioned.notes: Cobo is refuting the theory, popular among some 16th–17th century scholars, that Solomon's biblical fleet (possibly sailing to Ophir) had reached the Americas and that the indigenous peoples were descended from or influenced by Hebrews.

entities_mentioned.name: Hebrews [hebreos]
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Ancient Israelites/Jews as hypothetical pre-Columbian settlers of the Americas
entities_mentioned.notes: Part of the broader 'Lost Tribes of Israel' hypothesis applied to Amerindian origins, which Cobo systematically refutes in this chapter.

results.translator_notes:
{
  "title_inferred": "Inca Origin Myths: Manco-Cápac, the Sun, and the Founding of Cuzco",

De los demás sucesos de Tupa Inca Yupanqui

Entre las mujeres deste Inca había una de nación Guayro, de extremada hermosura, a la cual él amaba y favorecía más que a su legítima mujer, y tenía en ella un hijo igualmente amado que su madre; y no había fiestas y regocijos adonde no llevase consigo a madre y hijo. Dijo entonces a la Señora Guayro, que estaba presente: "Hermana, un punto me falta para ganar el juego; ¿qué haremos? Ella, aunque vió que la presea que se jugaba era de mucho valor, le respondió: "Ea, Señor, echa el dado en mi nombre, y di Guayro que Guayro será el as." Dijo el Inca: "Pues en tu nombre vaya, que se gane o se pierda." Y echando el dado el rey, dijeron a voces todos los presentes: "Guayro, Guayro." Y cuando cayó la suerte, fue el as, que era todo lo que se pedía. El Inca con todos los demás quedaron muy contentos, y mucho más la Señora, a quien dió luego el Inca la joya que ganó; y desde este juego mandó el Inca que el número uno se llamase guayro en toda la tierra, en memoria de la suerte y ganancia que con él hizo en nombre desta Señora; y así, hasta el día de hoy, en la mayor parte del reino llaman al número uno guayro, al modo que entre nosotros se dice as.

The Coya took this matter into her own hands and sent the inspector on his way, informing him of the course of action she intended, and instructing him to tell the cacica [chieftainess] that he had word from the Inca and the Coya that they wished to reserve that entire province for her, and that as a token of good tidings he should ask her to arrange a solemn celebration at sea.

All of this came to pass; and while the Indians were at sea with their musical instruments and great merriment, entirely unsuspecting of the inspector's cunning and deception, two of the Inca's captains entered the village and seized it. When the cacica and her subjects saw this from the sea, they had no recourse but to surrender. The captains arrested the cacica and brought her to present before the Coya.

The king spent four years on this royal inspection tour [visita]. He ordered the completion of the fortresses and palaces that had been begun in many places, and in others had many new ones built. He reached as far as the province of Quito, more than four hundred leagues from Cuzco, from where he returned to his court, having extended his kingdom on the northern side by more than twenty leagues, and along the coast and by sea, conquering all the valleys as far as Túmbez. Through such illustrious victories this Inca earned the name of "the Great," his people calling him the Great Tupa Inca, and indeed he truly was so. He died in the city of Cuzco, and his embalmed and well-preserved body remained intact until the imprisonment of Huascar-Inca, his grandson. At that time the captains of Atahuallpa — Quízquiz and Charcochima [sic] — burned it. He had an idol called Cuxichuri, to which the Indians paid great reverence, together with his ashes, which they kept stored in a jar [cántaro]. This king was very wealthy, and the aforementioned captains of Atahuallpa took much of his estate, while the remainder passed into the possession of his yanaconas [personal retainers/servants].

Entrados los españoles en esta tierra, hicieron grandes diligencias para descubrir su cuerpo, y aun no pocas violencias, por la fama de que tenía gran tesoro y que había de estar enterrado con su cuerpo o en los lugares que en vida más frecuentaba, porque ésta era costumbre antigua entre ellos. Al fin, por gran solicitud que se puso, y no con poco trabajo, fué hallado al tiempo que los cuerpos de los otros Incas. Hállose en el camino de la fortaleza, en una casa donde pareció haberle llevado la noche antes; que como los españoles iban ya por el rastro dándole alcance, los indios que lo guardaban lo mudaron a muchas partes, y con traerlo con tanta priesa y sobresaltos de unos lugares a otros, siempre lo mudaban con cinco o seis ídolos en su compañía, a quienes hacían gran veneración, porque estaban persuadidos que entendían en la guarda del cuerpo del Inca.

El ídolo principal que tuvo en vida se decía Guaraquinga; era grande y de oro puro, mas no estaba con su cuerpo ni pareció. Hicieron los indios grandes llantos en el descubrimiento, el cual se halló envuelto en muchas mantas de las muy finas y mucho algodón. Estaba entero y bien curado y conservado. Trújose a esta ciudad de Lima con los otros, y particularmente con el de su madre, que fué descubierto al mismo tiempo; y era tenido de los indios en grande estima, porque su hijo lo había introducido en mucha veneración y por su mandado se le hacían frecuentes sacrificios y tenía mucha gente de servicio. Estaba su cuerpo más bien curado que todos, porque no parecía estar muerto, y solos los ojos tenía postizos, tan bien hechos, que parecían naturales. Cuéntase comúnmente Guayna Cápac por el último rey de los Incas; lo uno por haber sido el postrero que poseyó el reino entero y pacífico; y lo otro porque muy poco después de su muerte entraron los españoles conquistando esta tierra y apoderándose della. De suerte que los Incas, que por algún tiempo ostentaron el título de reyes, lo fueron más que de sólo nombre.
results.translation:
The Indians of this king [said] that he was a great friend of the poor and ordered that great care be taken of them throughout his kingdom; that he was very affable with his people and drank more than three Indians combined, yet was never seen to lose his composure. When asked by his closest attendants how he could drink so much without becoming drunk, he replied that it was because he drank on behalf of the poor, for whose sustenance he greatly cared.

After the Spaniards entered this land, they made great efforts to discover his [mummified] body, and even resorted to no small amount of violence, on account of the repute that he had possessed great treasure that would have been buried with his body or in the places he had most frequented during his life, as this was an ancient custom among them. In the end, through great diligence and no little labor, it was found at the same time as the bodies of the other Incas. It was discovered on the road to the fortress, in a house to which it appeared to have been brought the night before; for as the Spaniards were already on the trail and closing in, the Indians who guarded it moved it to many different locations, and in transporting it with such haste and alarm from place to place, they always moved it in the company of five or six idols [huacas], to whom they showed great veneration, being persuaded that these idols were charged with guarding the body of the Inca.

The principal idol he had possessed in life was called Guaraquinga; it was large and made of pure gold, but it was not found with his body and never came to light. The Indians raised great lamentations at the discovery [of the body], which was found wrapped in many mantles of the finest quality and much cotton. It was intact and well cured [embalmed] and preserved. It was brought to this city of Lima along with the others, and in particular together with that of his mother, which was discovered at the same time; and it was held in great esteem by the Indians, because his son had introduced it into great veneration, and by his order frequent sacrifices were made to it, and it had many people in its service. His body was better preserved than all the others, for it did not appear to be dead; only his eyes were artificial [prosthetic], yet so well made that they appeared natural. Huayna Capac is commonly counted as the last king of the Incas: first, because he was the last to possess the kingdom whole and at peace; and second, because very shortly after his death the Spaniards entered, conquering this land and taking possession of it, so that those Incas who for some time thereafter bore the title of kings were so in little more than name only.
