En lo que toca a los gobernadores, magistrados y toda suerte de ministros de justicia, es muy poca la diversidad que hallamos en el gobierno de los Incas, porque, cuantos tenían alguna jurisdicción conocían en sus distritos de todo género de causas y delitos que no excediesen su potestad; de manera, que no se diferenciaban los gobernadores y jueces por las materias de que les era concedido juzgar o calidad de su jurisdicción sino por la cantidad y medida della, según a cada uno se la daba el rey. Unos la tenían muy corta y limitada, y otros muy amplia y cumplida; de modo, que para dar una regla general, basta decir, que como todos los vasallos se contaban y se les ponían superiores a ciertos números, aquellos tenían más amplia potestad que regían mayor número de indios. Comenzando, pues, de los más moderados y de menos autoridad, digo, que a cada diez indios tributarios o vecinos tenía puesto el Inca un superior que cuidaba de los nueve; y en cada cinco decurias déstas, otro que tenía cuenta con cincuenta; otro gobernaba una centuria, que constaba de las dos decurias de a cincuenta; en cada cinco centurias, o cada quinientos, había otro; y dos superiores de a quinientos con sus súbditos, reconocían a un milenario, que mandaba a mil; diez déstos estaban sujetos a otro más principal, que tenía debajo de su obediencia diez mil, que hacían una gobernación llamada hunu. Tomaban el nombre estos superiores del número de sus decurias: el que tenía cuenta con diez, se decía Chunca camayoc; el de quinientos, Pachacpachac camayoc; el de mil, Huaranca; y el de diez mil, Hunu; y todos los que gobernaban de ciento para arriba, se decían comúnmente curacas. Sobre todos éstos ponía el Inca en cada provincia un gobernador o virrey, el cual

results.title_inferred: Succession and Duties of Andean Chiefs (Caciques/Curacas) under Inca Administration
results.original_text:
ando a unos de lo que tenían, y añadiendo a otros; pero si alguno incurría en caso por el que mereciese ser privado del señorío que poseía, era echado dél y encomendábanlo a otro de la misma provincia, deudo del primero, si lo había. En la sucesión de los cacicazgos y señoríos guardaba el Inca este orden: que si el hijo mayor era hábil y capaz para el cacicazgo de su padre, lo nombraba en él y le daba el duho, que era una silleta baja o banquillo en que los caciques se asentaban para tomar la posesión del cacicazgo, y después solos ellos usaban este asiento; y si el mayor no era hábil y lo era el segundo, se daba a éste; y faltando hijos hábiles y de edad sucedía el hermano del cacique, y tenía el señorío mientras vivía; pero muerto éste, no le sucedía sino el sobrino que dejó de heredar por falta de edad competente: y cuando en todos los hijos de tal cacique no había ninguno suficiente para ello, ni dejaba el difunto hermanos, se nombraba por cacique la segunda persona del señorío, teniendo las partes y requisitos para serlo. Esto se guardaba en el nombramiento y sucesión de todos los curacas y caciques; los cuales proveía el Inca o el tocrícuc por especial misión suya; excepto los pachac-camayos de a cien indios y los de a menos, porque a éstos nombraban los guarancas o caciques de a mil súbditos a quienes eran sujetos, con consentimiento y aprobación de los caciques más principales; y no les podían quitar el oficio mientras vivían, si no era por delito grave, y entonces sucedían sus hijos como los demás. Los de a cincuenta y de a diez elegían los caciques y los quitaban cuando hacían mal su oficio, y no se heredaban.

Llamamos a estos señores caciques, porque con este nombre los comenzaron a llamar en la isla Española, que en las dos lenguas generales deste reino se nombran curacas en la quichua, y maycos en la aimará. Tenían todos estos superiores y mandones limitada jurisdicción, los menores más que los mayores, y los que más amplia la tenían, que eran los hinus, no podían dar pena de muerte; solamente conocían de los agravios pequeños y culpas livianas. Tenían cuidado de repartir las tierras de labor a sus súbditos, señalando a cada indio la cantidad que les bastaba; y asimismo el agua que había de tomar para regar sus chácaras y sementeras, si era tierra de regadío. Item, hacían sacar oro y plata los que tenían minas en sus distritos; una vez al año juntar en la plaza los mancebos y mujeres casaderas, y daban así a ellas como a ellos a escoger con quien querían casarse, y la que el hinu les daba recebían los indios por mujeres. Los decuriones inferiores tenían cuidado de ver cómo vivían los que estaban a su cargo, y de acusarlos cuando cometían algún delito. Otrosí, cuidaban de avisar de las necesidades que tenían a quien había de remediarlas; de contar los que nacían y morían; los mancebos y mujeres que llegaban a edad de casarse; los viejos y contrechos que se hallaban en sus decurias, para que en todo se diese orden como fuesen remediadas sus necesidades. De todas estas cosas daban cuenta estos decuriones a sus inmediatos superiores, y éstos la iban dando a sus mayores por sus grados hasta el Inca, que por esta forma sabía cada año los indios de todas edades que había en cada provincia para las contribuciones y mitas con que podían acudir para la guerra, obras públicas y demás ministerios en que solía ocuparlos.
results.translation:
taking away from some what they possessed, and adding to others; but if any one fell into a case whereby he deserved to be deprived of the lordship [señorío] he held, he was removed from it and it was entrusted to another person of the same province, a kinsman of the first, if one was available. In the succession of chieftainships [cacicazgos] and lordships, the Inca observed the following order: if the eldest son was capable and fit for his father's chieftainship, he was appointed to it and was given the duho [a low stool or small bench on which caciques sat to take possession of the chieftainship, and which thereafter only they used as a seat]; and if the eldest was not capable but the second son was, it was given to him; and in the absence of capable sons of suitable age, the brother of the cacique succeeded and held the lordship for his lifetime; but upon his death, it passed not to his own heir but to the nephew who had previously been passed over for want of sufficient age; and when among all the sons of such a cacique none was found sufficient for the office, and the deceased had left no brothers, the second-ranking person of the lordship was named as cacique, provided he possessed the qualities and requisites for the position. This was the rule observed in the appointment and succession of all curacas and caciques, who were appointed by the Inca or by the tocrícuc [regional governor/inspector] acting under special commission from him; excepting the pachac-camayos [overseers] of one hundred Indians and those of fewer, for these were appointed by the guarancas, or caciques of one thousand subjects to whom they were subordinate, with the consent and approval of the more principal caciques; and they could not be removed from office during their lifetime except for a serious crime, in which case their sons succeeded as in all other cases. Those overseeing groups of fifty and of ten were elected by the caciques and could be removed when they performed their office poorly, and their offices were not inherited.

We call these lords caciques because they began to be called by this name on the island of Hispaniola [Española]; in the two general languages of this kingdom they are called curacas in Quechua [quichua] and maycos in Aymara [aimará]. All these superiors and commanders had limited jurisdiction, the lesser ones more restricted than the greater, and those with the broadest jurisdiction—the hinus—could not impose the death penalty; they took cognizance only of minor grievances and light offenses. They were responsible for distributing the farmlands among their subjects, assigning to each Indian the amount sufficient for his needs; and likewise the water to be used for irrigating their fields [chácaras] and croplands [sementeras], where the land was irrigated. Furthermore, those who had mines in their districts caused gold and silver to be extracted from them; once a year they would assemble in the plaza the young men and marriageable women, giving both men and women the opportunity to choose whom they wished to marry, and the woman whom the hinu assigned to them the Indians received as wives. The inferior decurions [decuriones] were charged with observing how those under their care lived, and with denouncing them when they committed any offense. Moreover, they took care to report the needs of those under them to whoever was charged with remedying such needs; to count those who were born and who died; the young men and women who had reached marriageable age; the old and the infirm [contrechos] found within their decuries [decurias], so that provision might be made in all things for their needs to be met. Of all these matters the decurions gave an account to their immediate superiors, and these in turn reported to their own superiors through successive grades up to the Inca, who by this means knew each year the number of Indians of every age in each province, for the purposes of the tribute levies and labor drafts [mitas] by which they could be called upon for war, public works, and the other services in which he was accustomed to employ them.

entities_mentioned.name: duho
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Low ceremonial stool used in Andean and Caribbean chieftainship investiture ceremonies
entities_mentioned.notes: Cobo defines it explicitly as a low stool or small bench used by caciques at their investiture and as a symbol of their office thereafter. The term is also attested in Caribbean Taíno contexts.

entities_mentioned.name: cacique
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Indigenous chief or lord; term originating from Taíno (Hispaniola) and applied broadly across Spanish colonial America
entities_mentioned.notes: Cobo explicitly notes the term was first applied on Hispaniola and that the equivalent Quechua term is curaca and the Aymara term is mayco.

entities_mentioned.name: curaca
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Andean ethnic lord or chief; Quechua title
entities_mentioned.notes: Quechua equivalent of 'cacique' as used in the Inca imperial system.

entities_mentioned.name: mayco
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Aymara title for a lord or chief
entities_mentioned.notes: Aymara-language equivalent of curaca/cacique, as stated by Cobo.

entities_mentioned.name: tocrícuc
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Inca provincial governor or inspector (Quechua: Tokrikuq, 'he who sees all')
entities_mentioned.notes: A senior Inca administrative official with authority to appoint caciques by special commission of the Inca.

entities_mentioned.name: pachac-camayo
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Overseer responsible for a unit of one hundred tributary households in the Inca decimal administrative system
entities_mentioned.notes: From Quechua pachak (one hundred) and kamayoq (one in charge of). Part of the Inca hierarchical administrative system based on decimal groupings.

entities_mentioned.name: guaranca
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Inca administrative unit and its lord, comprising one thousand households; Quechua waranqa
entities_mentioned.notes: Cobo defines guarancas as 'caciques of a thousand subjects.' Part of the decimal hierarchy of Inca administration.

entities_mentioned.name: hinu
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Local Andean lord at a sub-provincial level; possibly equivalent to a lower-ranking curaca
entities_mentioned.notes: Cobo describes hinus as having the broadest jurisdiction among lower lords but still unable to impose the death penalty. The exact Quechua or Aymara etymology and precise rank within the hierarchy require further scholarly verification.

entities_mentioned.name: mita
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Andean rotational labor draft system (Quechua: mit'a)
entities_mentioned.notes: Obligatory labor service owed to the Inca state, used for war, public works, and other state purposes. Later adapted by Spanish colonial administration.

entities_mentioned.name: chácara
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Small cultivated field or farm plot (from Quechua chakra)
entities_mentioned.notes: Term still in widespread use across Andean countries today.

entities_mentioned.name: decurión / decuria
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Minor administrative officer and his unit, responsible for groups of ten households; term borrowed from Roman administrative vocabulary by Spanish authors
entities_mentioned.notes: Cobo uses Latin-derived terminology (decurion, decury) to describe the lowest-ranking officers in the Inca decimal system, i.e., those overseeing groups of ten.

entities_mentioned.name: isla Española
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Hispaniola (present-day Haiti and Dominican Republic)
entities_mentioned.notes: Cobo notes that the term 'cacique' was first introduced into Spanish usage on this island.

results.title_inferred: The Inca System of Collecting, Rearing, and Distributing Chosen Women (Acllas)
results.original_text:
juntaba era mucho mayor que el de los niños, como se echa de ver por los ministerios a que las destinaban. El modo que había en recogerlas era éste: a cada provincia se despachaba un juez o comisario nombrado por el Inca, que sólo entendía en este negocio de recoger niñas, guardarlas y enviarlas al Cuzco cuando estaban de edad; y llamábase Apupanaca; el cual, discurriendo por los pueblos de su jurisdicción, tenía potestad de señalar todas las que a él le pareciesen hermosas y de buena traza y disposición, desde ocho o nueve años para abajo, a las cuales llamaban Acllas, que es tanto como escogidas, y había en cada cabecera de gobernación la suya. Criábanse allí hasta los catorce años en compañía de las mamaconas, que eran las mujeres recogidas y dedicadas al servicio de sus dioses al modo de monjas, o de las vírgenes vestales de Roma; las cuales enseñaban a estas niñas todas las obras y ejercicios mujeriles, como hilar y tejer lana y algodón, guisar de comer, hacer sus vinos o chichas, con los otros ministerios que pertenecen a mujeres. Había, para sustentarlas, señalada renta de las heredades consagradas a la religión, y mayordomos que entendiesen en proveerlas de lo necesario y en guardarlas con toda vigilancia, a fin de que se conservasen doncellas.

El comisario que escogía este tributo sacaba en cada un año para la fiesta del Raymi destas casas de recogimiento las que llegaban a trece o catorce años, y de ahí para arriba, y con no menos guarda que hasta allí habían tenido las llevaba al Cuzco, conforme al número que a cada provincia cabía enviar aquel año. Juntas en aquella ciudad las que remitían todas las provincias, que eran en número excesivo, las ponían en la presencia del Inca, el cual las repartía luego, conforme a la necesidad presente, por este orden: unas aplicaba a los monasterios de mamaconas para enterar el número de las que morían, y éstas profesaban aquel estado, viviendo perpetuamente en clausura y castidad, ocupadas en servicio de los templos del sol, del trueno y de los otros dioses que tenían servicio de mujeres.

Otro buen número apartaba y mandaba guardar para matar en los sacrificios que se hacían en el discurso del año, que eran muchos y por diferentes respetos, como por la salud del Inca, cuando enfermaba o cuando iba en persona a la guerra; y para, si muriese, matar las que habían de enviar a la otra vida en su compañía, o para muchas otras ocasiones que el demonio les inducía tener necesidad de este sacrificio; en el cual era requisito necesario que estuviesen vírgenes. Las más nobles y hermosas señalaba para criadas y mancebas suyas, y gran cantidad de ellas repartía entre sus capitanes y parientes, remunerando con este género de premio los servicios que le hacían; y con este intento daba también destas doncellas a otras personas a quienes tenía voluntad de hacer merced; y recibir una doncella déstas de su mano era tenido por favor singular; porque ninguna cosa estimaban tanto estos indios como tener muchas mujeres; los cuales, después de la legítima, no podían tener otra sino por merced del rey, y solíala dar por diferentes respetos, como si dijésemos, por ser alguno eminente en alguna arte, o haber dado alguna industria en cosas tocantes al bien público, o por haber hecho alguna proeza en la guerra.

A los gobernadores también solía conceder facultad el Inca para repartir algunas entre los caciques y principales de cada provincia de donde se sacaban; finalmente, era muy grande el número de mujeres que se mandaban recoger para estos efectos, sin tenerse respeto a cuyas hijas eran, sino a la elección y disposición del Apupanaca, ni poder reclamar los padres por razón alguna, ni mostrar tristeza porque se las llevasen. En este género de contribución no había tasa ni número determinado, como ni en las demás, y así no era igual la cantidad de las que se recogían cada año. Con ser un tributo éste tan duro y pesado, lo hacía llevadero en parte el creído los indios que las doncellas que mataban en los sacrificios hechos en honra de sus dioses, por la salud del rey o por otras necesidades de la república, iban sus ánimas a tener grandísimo descanso; y esto solía ser ocasión de que las ofreciesen algunos padres de su voluntad, mayormente en el Cuzco y su comarca, cuando el Inca o algún señor grande adolecía: si bien es verdad que lo más ordinario era lo que de su cosecha lleva el amor de padres, esto es, recebir pena y dolor, como en hecho de verdad la tenían muy grande de verse privar de prendas tan caras y conjuntas con sus propias entrañas, y que se les quitase el dominio de la patria potestad sobre sus hijos, y se los arrebatasen a pesar suyo, y delante de sus ojos los entregasen a la muerte, cuando apenas habían comenzado a gozar desta luz de vida; y esta era la principal razón de la poca guarda que tenían con sus hijas, antes afirman que holgaban de verlas corrompidas sin tiempo; porque, de esa suerte, estaban seguras del Apupanaca, por ser requisito sin el cual no las escogía, el estar vírgenes; y esto no con más intento que de servirse dellas en tanto que tenían edad para casarse; porque tampoco en esto tenían los padres ni ellas libertad.
results.translation:
The number [of girls collected] was much greater than that of boys, as can be seen from the duties to which they were assigned. The manner of collecting them was as follows: to each province there was dispatched a judge or commissioner appointed by the Inca, whose sole charge was the business of gathering girls, keeping them, and sending them to Cuzco when they were of age; he was called the Apupanaca. Traveling through the towns of his jurisdiction, he had the authority to designate all those who seemed to him beautiful and of good form and bearing, from eight or nine years of age and younger, whom they called Acllas—meaning, as much as to say, 'chosen ones'—and each provincial capital had its own such official. The girls were raised there until the age of fourteen in the company of the mamaconas, who were cloistered women dedicated to the service of their gods after the manner of nuns, or of the Vestal Virgins of Rome; these women taught the girls all the works and exercises proper to women, such as spinning and weaving wool and cotton, cooking food, making their wines or chichas [maize beer], along with the other duties that belong to women. For their sustenance, a designated income was set aside from the estates consecrated to religion, and stewards were appointed to see to their provisioning with what was necessary and to guard them with all vigilance, so that they might be kept as maidens.

The commissioner who selected this tribute drew out each year, for the feast of Raymi [Inti Raymi, the Inca festival of the sun], from these houses of seclusion those who had reached thirteen or fourteen years of age and older, and with no less guard than they had hitherto been kept under, he conducted them to Cuzco, in accordance with the number that each province was required to send that year. Once assembled in that city—those sent by all the provinces being an exceedingly large number—they were brought before the Inca, who then distributed them according to present need, in the following manner: some he assigned to the monasteries of mamaconas to fill the places of those who had died, and these professed that state, living perpetually in enclosure and chastity, occupied in the service of the temples of the sun, of the thunder, and of the other gods whose worship required female attendants.

Another considerable number he set apart and ordered kept for sacrifice in the rites performed throughout the course of the year, which were many and for various reasons—such as for the health of the Inca when he fell ill, or when he went in person to war; and so that, should he die, those who were to be sent to the other world in his company might be slain; or for many other occasions which the demon [i.e., the devil, in Cobo's framing] prompted them to regard as requiring this sacrifice—for which it was a necessary condition that the victims be virgins. The noblest and most beautiful he designated as his own servants and concubines, and a great number he distributed among his captains and kinsmen, rewarding with this kind of prize the services they had rendered him; and with this same intention he also gave some of these maidens to other persons whom he wished to favor; and to receive one of these maidens from his hand was regarded as a singular honor, because nothing was so highly prized by these Indians as having many wives. After the legitimate wife, a man could have no other except by the king's grant, and this was customarily given for various reasons—as, for example, if a man were eminent in some craft, or had contributed some useful invention in matters pertaining to the public good, or had performed some feat of valor in war.

The Inca also customarily granted governors the authority to distribute some of these women among the caciques [native lords] and leading men of each province from which they had been drawn. In sum, the number of women ordered to be collected for these purposes was very great, without any regard being paid to whose daughters they were, but only to the selection and determination of the Apupanaca; nor could fathers protest on any grounds, nor show sadness that their daughters were taken from them. In this form of tribute there was no fixed rate or set number, as there was none in the other tributes either, and so the quantity of girls collected each year was not uniform. Harsh and burdensome as this tribute was, it was made partly bearable by the Indians' belief that the souls of the maidens killed in sacrifices offered in honor of their gods—for the health of the king or for other needs of the realm—went to a state of the greatest rest and happiness; and this was sometimes occasion for some fathers to offer them of their own free will, especially in Cuzco and its environs, when the Inca or some great lord fell ill. Yet it is true that what was more ordinarily the case was what paternal love naturally brings, namely grief and pain, for in truth they felt it very deeply to see themselves deprived of pledges so dear and so joined to their very entrails, to have the authority of parental power over their children taken from them, to have them snatched away against their will and delivered before their eyes to death, when they had scarcely begun to enjoy the light of life. And this was the principal reason why they kept little watch over their daughters—indeed, it is affirmed that they were glad to see them corrupted before their time, because in that way they were safe from the Apupanaca, since being a virgin was a requirement without which he would not select them; and this was done with no other intention than to make use of the girls until they were of marriageable age, for in this matter too neither the parents nor the girls themselves had any freedom.

entities_mentioned.name: Apupanaca (also spelled Apupcmacya / Apupanaca in source)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Apu panaca or similar Quechua title; the imperial official (sometimes called 'apupanaca') charged with selecting aclla women for the Inca state
entities_mentioned.notes: Cobo's spelling is inconsistent across the passage (Apupcmacya, Apupanaca, Apupanaca); the OCR introduces further variation. The role corresponds to what other sources call the 'apu' or inspector charged with the aclla system.

entities_mentioned.notes: Described here as one of the beverages the acllas were trained to produce.

entities_mentioned.name: Cuzco

results.original_text:
untó en vida; que como el entraba a reinar no tocaba a la hacienda y riquezas de su predecesor, sino aplicado aquello a la guarda y servicio del difunto, el nuevo rey ponía casa de nuevo y juntaba de por sí plata y oro con todo lo demás, era inmenso el tesoro que en aquella ciudad había, a pelo de haber procurado cada rey aventajar su casa en riqueza, lustre y aparato a las de sus antecesores. Así, por esto, como por estar allí los ricos y frecuentes templos de todo el reino y las guacas y dioses principales de las provincias con el insigne y tan venerado santuario del sol, llamado Coricancha, que quiere decir casa del oro, y era de los más ricos de oro y plata que ha habido en el mundo, adonde acudían de todas partes con lo más precioso que tenían a ofrecer sus votos y sacrificios, fue aquella ciudad la más rica que se ha hallado en todo este Nuevo Mundo; y la razón de haberse juntado en ella tan gran riqueza de plata y oro y piedras preciosas, fue, porque de todas estas cosas hacían presentes al Inca los caciques y gobernadores cuando lo visitaban en su corte o cuando él iba a las tierras dellos visitando su reino. E íbase acrecentando cada día más esta riqueza, por ser las provincias muchas e irse continuamente reduciendo otras a su obediencia, y también porque eran muy pocos los que usaban destos metales, excepto los grandes señores y los caballeros, que en la guerra se adornaban con algunas joyas hechas de oro y plata; y así, casi todo cuanto desto se sacaba de las muchas minas que había, y hoy en día hay por todo el reino, venía a poder del Inca; las cuales minas unas se labraban a costa y por cuenta del mismo Inca, y otras, que eran las más, hacían labrar a sus expensas los caciques de los distritos en que caían, por tener de qué hacerle presentes. Allegábase a esto la prohibición que había de que nadie pudiese sacar plata ni oro del Cuzco; con que vinieron a ser incomparables los tesoros que de mucho tiempo estaban allí juntos y amontonados para grandeza y autoridad de los reyes; y no sólo de la dicha ciudad no había saca para otras partes del reino, pero ni de todo él se sacaba deste género para otros extraños, ni se gastaba en cosas que se consumen con el uso, como gastamos nosotros, más que en ídolos, figuras, láminas, vasos y preseas para servicio y ornato de los templos y del rey y grandes señores; ni tampoco los reyes hacían las pagas ordinarias a sus criados y ministros en estos metales preciosos, sino en la ropa y comidas que le contribuían los pueblos. De todo lo cual se deja bien entender la incomparable riqueza que en tantos siglos habían allegado y recogido los Incas, y que no fue sino muy pequeña parte lo que destos tesoros vino a poder de los españoles, con haber sido en tanta cantidad; porque lo más ocultaron y enterraron los indios, viendo la ansia con que los españoles andaban en busca destos metales y la grande estima que de ellos hacían.

Finalmente, servía por su turno al Inca una de sus hermanas con mucho número de hijas de señores, que eran damas destas señoras y se mudaban cada ocho días. Estas señoras le asistían siempre para servirle; porque los criados y gentiles hombres no entraban dentro de la sala donde él residía, sino que se estaban fuera en los patios, y en llamando a alguno, entraba descalzo a su presencia.
results.translation:
anointed in life; for as he who came to reign did not touch the estate and riches of his predecessor — all of that being set apart for the maintenance and service of the deceased — the new king established his household anew and accumulated silver and gold and all else for himself. The treasure in that city was immense, owing to the fact that each king had endeavored to surpass his predecessors in the wealth, splendor, and magnificence of his household. In addition to this, the city contained the rich and numerous temples of the entire kingdom, the huacas [sacred shrines] and principal gods of the provinces, and the renowned and greatly venerated sanctuary of the Sun, called Coricancha — meaning 'house of gold' — which was among the richest in gold and silver that has ever existed in the world, and to which people came from all parts bearing their most precious possessions to offer their vows and sacrifices. For all these reasons, that city was the richest ever found in all this New World. The reason so great a wealth of silver, gold, and precious stones accumulated there was that the caciques [local lords] and governors made gifts of all these things to the Inca whenever they visited him at his court, or when he traveled through their lands on progress through his kingdom. And this wealth grew greater every day, both because the provinces were many and others were continually being brought under his dominion, and also because very few people made use of these metals, except for the great lords and the warriors, who adorned themselves in battle with certain jewels made of gold and silver. Thus, nearly everything extracted from the many mines that existed — and still exist today throughout the kingdom — came into the possession of the Inca. Some of these mines were worked at the Inca's own cost and on his own account, while the others — the greater number — were worked at the expense of the caciques of the districts in which they were located, since those lords needed something with which to make him gifts. Added to this was the prohibition against anyone removing silver or gold from Cuzco, so that the treasures that had been accumulating and amassing there over so long a time, for the grandeur and authority of the kings, became incomparable. Not only was there no export of these metals from that city to other parts of the kingdom, but none was taken from the entire kingdom to outsiders; nor was it spent on things that are consumed through use, as we spend ours, except on idols, figures, sheets [of metal], vessels, and ornaments for the service and adornment of the temples, the king, and the great lords. Nor did the kings make ordinary payments to their servants and ministers in these precious metals, but rather in the cloth and foodstuffs contributed by the towns. From all of this one may readily understand the incomparable wealth that the Incas had accumulated and gathered over so many centuries, and that only a very small portion of these treasures came into the hands of the Spaniards, even though that portion was of such great quantity — for the greater part was hidden and buried by the Indians, seeing the eagerness with which the Spaniards went about searching for these metals and the great value they placed upon them.

Finally, one of the Inca's sisters served him in rotation, attended by a great number of daughters of lords, who were ladies-in-waiting to these noblewomen and who were replaced every eight days. These noblewomen were always in attendance to serve him; for the male servants and gentlemen of the court did not enter the hall where he resided, but remained outside in the courtyards, and when any one of them was called, he entered barefoot into the Inca's presence.

results.title_inferred: On Inca Religious Devotion, Sacrifice, and the Preservation of Religious Memory
results.original_text:
Bernabe Cobo

clían en sus guacas, y las hacían creer al simple vulgo, para que, creciendo la reputación de sus dioses, se acrecentasen las ofrendas; y estaban recibidas y asentadas algunas cosas tan aparentes y sin fundamento, que no es posible que algunos dejasen de mirar en ellas y conociesen su falsedad y engaño, siendo tan claras y demostrativas, sino que, cuando una cosa está introducida, aunque sea notoriamente mala, ninguno, aunque lo alcance, osa ser el primero que lo contradiga. Porque claro está que muchos filósofos alcanzaron ser burla la multitud de los dioses, y con todo eso no osaban hablar en ello, por no contradecir a la multitud de la gente. Lo cual especialmente ha más lugar entre estos indios, en quien la libertad era poca, los castigos grandes y mucha la gente que se mantenía destos oficios.

Tenía el demonio a esta ciega gente tan edificada y habituada en su errada seta, particularmente a los Incas (que era la nación más dada a la religión que debe haber habido en el mundo), que hacían sus ritos y sacrificios con tanta determinación y en tanta cantidad, que casi cuanto cogían y cuanto trabajaban y hasta sus propios hijos consumían en ello; y así, de tan profunda devoción como mostraban y cuidado que cada uno ponía en venerar y sacrificar lo que estaba a su cargo de una religión tan arraigada y universalmente recibida y de una determinación que pone espanto de ofrecer y sacrificar a sus propios hijos y haciendas, matándolos y quemándolos, conforme a sus costumbres, no se puede presumir que sus actos fuesen solamente exteriores, pues no permite la naturaleza que uno mate a su mismo hijo y aventure su hacienda con ánimo y voluntad tan alegre, si no tuviese fines y pretensiones de galardón por lo que hace, o pensase y tuviese creído enviaba su hijo a mejor lugar que el que acá tenía; y para dar muestras exteriores de contento cuando lo hace, necesario es que interiormente lo crea y que tenga por cierto que no se pierde el sacrificio. Y así no hay duda sino que las obras déstos pendían de alguna esperanza, pues fuera bestialidad matar nadie sus hijos y consumir su hacienda si no creyese que le aprovechaba para algo.

Puesto caso que todas las naciones deste reino del Perú tuvieron fines y cuenta con sus dioses y adoratorios, ninguna hubo que llegase con mucho a preciarse de tan religiosa como los Incas; y así eran ellos los más cargados de ceremonias, supersticiones, ídolos y sacrificios, y guardaban con tanta observancia lo estatuido en orden a esto, que eran leyes y opiniones inviolables entre ellos; y aunque el delito que contra ellas se cometía, y aun el descuido en la veneración de lo estatuido, fuera castigado ásperamente, fue tanta su religión, que por falta della dicen haberse castigado muy pocos, aunque con gran diligencia se tuvo con ello cuenta; que cierto no es de pasar por esto fácilmente, pues al que le cabía sacrificar el hijo, aunque no tuviese otro, era delito notable el mostrar alguna tristeza, sino que lo hacían con muestras de alegría y contentamiento, como si los llevaran a dar un cargo de grande importancia.

Lo que en esta materia de religión más admira, es la cuenta tan extraordinaria que tenían estos indios en conservar la memoria de las cosas tocantes a ella. Porque dado que no las tenían por escrito para saberlas y guardarlas, suplían esta falta con aprenderlas y guardarlas por tradición tan exactamente, que parece las tenían esculpidas en los huesos. Porque para sólo este efecto tenían los Incas puestos en la ciudad del Cuzco más de mil hombres que no entendían en otra cosa más que en la conservación desta memoria; y con éstos se criaban otros desde mancebos, que eran instruidos dellos, para que no se pudiese perder; que bien creo yo que semejante cuidado no se halló en los gentiles antiguos ni en otra ninguna gente para conservar su religión y memoria de sus opiniones y adoratorios. Estos que entendían en este ministerio eran comúnmente viejos y sacerdotes o ministros de sus templos, y hacíanlo con tanto cuidado, que cuando eran preguntados, uno por sí daba razón y declaraba la virtud que decía tener la guaca que estaba a su cargo, las solemnidades y palabras con que se le había de hacer el sacrificio, y la ofrenda que se le había de dar, prometiendo gran esperanza del buen suceso con la relación que hacía de otros semejantes que para acreditar su adoratorio fingía. Y si bien es verdad que todas las razones que apartan del camino verdadero de Dios son débiles y de ningún fundamento, todavía en muchas cosas las daban estos indios más coloreadas para sus yerros que los gentiles filósofos y poetas. Verdad es que no se les podía pedir entera razón de todo, porque no la alcanzaban, ni sabían los fundamentos en que estribaban para sus opiniones; pues de lo más tenían por principal causa la costumbre de sus antepasados, la cual tuvieron por ley inviolable en muchas cosas en tanto grado, que de algunas no sabían dar otra razón, y si daban alguna, puesto caso que en la forma, solemnidad y supersticiones estaban conformes, en ella diferenciaban mucho; porque, como no tenían escrituras, perdíase la razón y motivo de los pasados, y después tenían las cosas en la opinión que las hallaron, y cada uno inventaba lo que le parecía; en lo cual los que reparaban eran los menos y gente estimada y noble; que la común no se ponía a particularizar razones, sino que seguía en general a éstos y hacía lo que le mandaban y veía que se usaba. Y así aun los mismos que no trataban de otra cosa que de lo tocante a su religión, no tenían memoria del principio de las más de sus opiniones y devaneos. Y sin duda efecto de ser muy de atrás, porque tantas cosas no se [ILLEGIBLE: text breaks off mid-sentence]
results.translation:
They proclaimed [these things] in their huacas [sacred shrines or oracles], and made the simple common people believe them, so that, as the reputation of their gods grew, the offerings would increase. Certain things had been accepted and established that were so specious and without foundation that it is impossible that some people did not look into them and recognize their falsity and deception—being so clear and demonstrable—except that when something has been introduced, even if it is notoriously wrong, no one, even if he perceives it, dares to be the first to contradict it. For it is plain that many philosophers came to understand that the multitude of gods was a mockery, and yet for all that they dared not speak of it, so as not to contradict the mass of the people. This applies with even greater force among these Indians, among whom freedom was little, punishments were great, and many people depended on these offices for their livelihood.

The devil had this blind people so thoroughly indoctrinated and habituated in their erroneous sect—particularly the Incas (who were the nation most given to religion of any that can ever have existed in the world)—that they performed their rites and sacrifices with such resolve and in such quantity that they consumed in them nearly all they harvested, all they labored for, and even their own children. Given the depth of devotion they displayed, and the care each one took in venerating and sacrificing what fell within his charge, in a religion so deeply rooted and universally received—and given the terrifying resolve to offer and sacrifice their own children and possessions, killing and burning them according to their customs—one cannot suppose that their acts were merely outward; for nature does not permit a man to kill his own child and hazard his estate with such a willing and cheerful spirit, unless he had aims and expectations of reward for what he does, or unless he believed and held it for certain that he was sending his child to a better place than the one he had here. And in order to show outward signs of contentment when he does this, it is necessary that he inwardly believe it and hold for certain that the sacrifice is not in vain. There is therefore no doubt that the deeds of these people depended upon some hope, for it would have been mere bestiality to kill one's children and consume one's estate if one did not believe it would profit something.

Although all the nations of this kingdom of Peru had regard for their gods and their shrines [adoratorios], none came anywhere near priding itself on being as religious as the Incas; and thus they were the most burdened with ceremonies, superstitions, idols, and sacrifices, and they observed what had been ordained in this regard with such strictness that these were inviolable laws and principles among them. And although the crime committed against them—and even mere negligence in the veneration of what had been ordained—would have been harshly punished, their religiosity was so great that they say very few were punished for want of it, even though diligent account was kept of this matter. This is certainly not to be passed over lightly, for one to whom it fell to sacrifice a child—even if he had no other—committed a notable offense by showing any sadness; rather, they did it with outward signs of joy and contentment, as if they were sending them to take up a post of great importance.

What most astonishes in this matter of religion is the extraordinary care these Indians took in preserving the memory of things pertaining to it. For, given that they did not have them in writing in order to know and preserve them, they supplied this deficiency by learning and keeping them through oral tradition so exactly that they seemed to have them engraved upon their very bones. For this purpose alone, the Incas stationed in the city of Cuzco more than a thousand men who occupied themselves with nothing other than the preservation of this memory; and alongside these, other young men were raised from boyhood who were instructed by them, so that the knowledge could not be lost. I well believe that such care was not found among the ancient pagans [gentiles] nor among any other people for the purpose of preserving their religion and the memory of their beliefs and shrines. Those who attended to this ministry were commonly old men and priests or ministers of their temples, and they carried it out with such care that when questioned, each one individually would give an account and declare the virtue that he said the huaca in his charge possessed, the solemnities and words with which sacrifice was to be made to it, and the offering that was to be given to it, promising great hope of a favorable outcome by recounting other similar cases that he fabricated in order to lend credibility to his shrine. And although it is true that all the arguments that lead away from the true path of God are weak and without foundation, nevertheless in many matters these Indians gave their errors a more plausible coloring than the pagan philosophers and poets gave theirs. It is true that one could not expect a full account of everything from them, because they did not attain it, nor did they know the foundations upon which their beliefs rested; for in most things they held the customs of their ancestors to be the principal cause, and regarded this as an inviolable law in so many matters that for some things they could give no other reason, and if they did give one, although they agreed in form, solemnity, and superstition, they differed greatly in substance; for since they had no writings, the reasoning and motive of those who had gone before was lost, and afterward they held things in the estimation in which they found them, each one inventing what seemed fitting to him. Among these, those who gave the matter careful thought were the fewest in number and were the esteemed and noble people; the common people did not trouble themselves to particularize reasons, but rather followed these persons in general and did what they commanded and saw practiced. And so even those whose sole occupation was matters pertaining to their religion had no memory of the origin of most of their beliefs and notions. And doubtless this was an effect of their being of very ancient standing, because so many things could not [ILLEGIBLE: text breaks off mid-sentence]

entities_mentioned.notes: Cobo uses the term to refer both to the physical shrine and to the deity or sacred power associated with it. The priests in charge of individual huacas were responsible for maintaining their ritual prescriptions and oral traditions.

entities_mentioned.notes: Cobo distinguishes the Incas as the most religiously observant of all Andean peoples, singling them out for the intensity and institutional organization of their religious practice.

entities_mentioned.name: Cuzco

entities_mentioned.notes: Identified as the location where the Incas stationed over a thousand men dedicated solely to the preservation of religious oral tradition.

results.translator_notes:
1. The segment as supplied contains no plant, drug, or medical material; it is a theological and ethnographic commentary on Inca religion, sacrifice, and oral tradition. No medical entities are therefore extracted.
2. 'Guaca' (huaca) is preserved in brackets at first occurrence and thereafter as 'huaca' in translation, as it is an established scholarly term.
3. The phrase 'enviaba su hijo a mejor lugar' ('he was sending his son to a better place') is a notable rhetorical move by Cobo, attributing to the Incas something resembling a belief in an afterlife or blessed state for sacrificial victims.
4. The text breaks off mid-sentence at the end of the supplied segment ('porque tantas cosas no se...'); this is marked [ILLEGIBLE: text breaks off mid-sentence] as the truncation appears to be a supply/digitization issue rather than an OCR problem.
5. 'Bestialidad' (bestiality/brutishness) is translated as 'mere bestiality' to capture its rhetorical force as an extreme moral condemnation.
6. OCR artifact '>' (HTML entity for '>') silently removed from 'cargo' in the phrase 'como si los llevaran a dar un cargo de grande importancia.'
7. 'Seta' in context means 'sect' (religious faction/practice), not 'mushroom' — translated accordingly.
8. The passage 'pues fuera bestialidad' contains the OCR rendering 'Ijestialidaíj' — silently corrected to 'bestialidad' as unambiguous.

results.title_inferred: Inca Origins, Flood Myths, Ancestor Shrines, and Beliefs about the Soul and Afterlife
results.original_text:
capítulo III, los cuales veneraban como adoratorio insigne la cueva de Pacaritampu, por haberse escapado allí de la destrucción del mundo sus progenitores; de los cuales tenían creído que se había poblado la tierra y que todas las gentes descendían dellos. Aunque también discordaban, porque unos dicen que los Incas que salieron de aquella cueva los puso Dios allí; otros que no, sino que, cuando vino el diluvio, se metieron en ella y taparon bien la puerta, con que escaparon de las aguas. Estos y otros desatinos semejantes contaban, así los Incas como las otras naciones deste reino, que por evitar prolijidad no los pongo aquí, pues para el intento bastan los referidos. Lo cual todo causó en estas ciegas gentes el no haber conocido al verdadero Dios, y haberse dado sin rienda a toda suerte de vicios; a que ayudó su parte el haber carecido de letras, que si las hubieran tenido, pudiera ser no tuvieran tan ciegas y tan torpes errores.

Lo que destas sus ficciones y novelas yo saco a propósito deste discurso es que dellas tuvieron origen muchísimos adoratorios y guacas, cada provincia el suyo, que fué el lugar donde entendían haberse salvado aquellos que tenían por principio y cepa de cada nación; y eran estos lugares en cada provincia muy conocidos y venerados con toda suerte de sacrificios. El fin que en la adoración dellos tenían, era la conservación y propagación de la gente de la dicha provincia. Asimismo desta opinión tuvieron principio los dioses principales y patrones de las provincias, que eran aquellos que concebían haber escapado del diluvio en los lugares sobredichos; y aunque es verdad que en ninguna parte tuvieron los cuerpos dellos, mas como fueron imaginaciones, halló también el demonio camino para hacerles entender que, cumplido el tiempo de su vida, que decían haber sido muy larga, se habían convertido en piedras, y a éstas en lugar de sus cuerpos tenían en veneración y les ofrecían sacrificios.

El fundamento de tantos disparates que vinieron a creer todas estas naciones del Perú acerca de su origen fué causado por la ambición de los Incas, que fueron los primeros que hicieron veneración a la cueva de Pacaritampu como a principio de su generación; y como afirmasen que della habían procedido todas las gentes, y que por esta causa les debían sujeción y vasallaje y se lo debían dar, y llevasen por delante este título en sus conquistas, vino a resultar de aquí esta muchedumbre de adoratorios y dioses, en cada provincia el suyo, de la misma dedicación que los Incas habían dado al de Pacaritampu; y no negando el diluvio, se procuraban excusar de sujetarse a los Incas, defendiéndose con decir que no los debían reconocer por esa razón, de haber procedido dellos. Porque después del diluvio había habido gente nueva en cada provincia, de donde se tornó a poblar la tierra, en la cual opinión les pusieron sus viejos y hechiceros; y para darle fuerza y establecimiento, les señalaron los lugares donde y como se habían salvado sus progenitores. Y como los Incas respetaban la cueva de Pacaritampu, también los otros hicieron veneración al lugar que señalaron para el efecto dicho, aunque no con tanta orden ni con sacrificios tan principales; y así, en cualquiera provincia se halla este adoratorio, cada uno con su imaginación, contando el caso cada nación diferentemente.

CAPÍTULO III

De las opiniones que tuvieron estos indios en lo que toca a las ánimas y otra vida después désta

Acerca deste punto tuvieron muchas opiniones diferentes unas de otras; en lo que concordaban todos (a lo que se puede alcanzar) sin discrepar ninguno, es en dos cosas sustanciales: la primera, en conocer la inmortalidad del alma y que en el hombre hay más de lo que se ve con los ojos; y la otra, en que el bueno tiene gloria y el malo pena después desta vida. Mostraban lo primero en el cuidado grande que tenían de prevenir y adornar sus sepulturas; en meter en ellas con el difunto todos sus haberes, mujeres y servicio y cantidad de comida y bebida; y lo segundo, en la enseñanza, exhortaciones y reprehensiones que los sacerdotes y maestros suyos daban al común del pueblo, para apartarlos de lo malo e inclinarlos a lo bueno, unas veces amenazándolos con el castigo y otras convidándolos con el premio de la otra vida; dado caso que en la forma que esta gloria y pena se da y recibe difieren y desatinan, en lo cual era imposible acertasen sin lumbre de fe.

Algunos creían que, salida el alma del cuerpo, si había vivido bien, se hacía estrella, y que de allí procedían todas las del cielo, y que allí gozaban de gloria. Y si la vida había sido mala, iba a cierto lugar donde tenía pena perpetua; la cual dónde y cómo se la daban también discrepaban, y cada uno fingía lo que quería: porque no tenían en esto cosa fija ni asentada ni obligatoria, sino que, como gente sin lumbre, andaban vacilando e inventando cada día cosas nuevas, conforme a la flaqueza humana. Otros tenían que las ánimas que salían de los cuerpos de unas partes, venían a nacer en otras, y que cuando del todo saliesen de esta vida (que fin afirmaban que ha de tener el mundo) recibirían gloria o pena, según sus méritos. Otras naciones pensaban que las ánimas de los difuntos se quedaban en este mundo, y que unas veces tenían gozo, y otras eran afligidas, y andaban vagas y solitarias, padeciendo hambre, sed, frío, calor y cansancio; y que ellas o sus fantasmas solían visitar a sus parientes y a otras personas, en señal que habían de morir o les había de venir algún mal: y por esta razón de creer que las ánimas tenían hambre, sed y otros trabajos, ofrecían en las sepulturas comidas, bebidas, ropa y otras cosas, para que aprovechasen a los difuntos; y po[...]
results.translation:
Chapter III — [concerning] those who venerated as a celebrated shrine [adoratorio insigne] the cave of Pacaritampu, because their ancestors had there escaped the destruction of the world; and they believed that the earth had been peopled from those ancestors, and that all peoples were descended from them. They also disagreed among themselves on this point, for some say that God placed there in that cave the Incas who emerged from it; others deny this, saying rather that when the flood came, they entered the cave and sealed its door fast, and so escaped the waters. These and other similar absurdities were recounted both by the Incas and by the other nations of this realm; I do not set them all down here in order to avoid prolixity, since those already mentioned are sufficient for my purpose. All of this was caused in these benighted peoples by their failure to know the true God, and by their having given themselves without restraint to every sort of vice; a condition furthered in no small part by their lack of letters [writing], for had they possessed these, it is possible they would not have fallen into errors so blind and so gross.

What I draw from these their fictions and tales for the purpose of this discussion is that from them arose a very great number of shrines [adoratorios] and huacas [guacas], each province having its own, which was the place where they understood that those whom they regarded as the origin and stock [cepa] of each nation had been saved; and these places in each province were well known and venerated with all manner of sacrifices. The purpose they had in worshipping them was the preservation and propagation of the people of the said province. Likewise from this belief arose the principal gods and patron deities of the provinces, who were those they imagined to have escaped the flood in the aforementioned places; and although in truth the bodies of these ancestors were nowhere to be found, yet since they were mere imaginings, the Devil also found a way to make the people understand that, once the term of their lives — which they said had been very long — was fulfilled, these ancestors had been transformed into stones, and these stones they held in veneration in place of their bodies, and offered sacrifices to them.

The foundation of all the absurdities that all these nations of Peru came to believe concerning their origins was caused by the ambition of the Incas, who were the first to venerate the cave of Pacaritampu as the origin of their lineage; and as they affirmed that all peoples had issued from it, and that for this reason those peoples owed them subjection and vassalage and were obliged to render it, and as they advanced this claim in their conquests, there resulted from this the multitude of shrines and gods, each province having its own, with the same dedication that the Incas had given to that of Pacaritampu. Not denying the flood, the other peoples sought to excuse themselves from subjection to the Incas, defending themselves by saying they owed them no such recognition on the grounds of having descended from them — because after the flood there had been new people in each province, from whom the land had been repopulated again. In this belief their elders and sorcerers [hechiceros] had instructed them; and in order to lend it force and establishment, the elders designated the places where and how their ancestors had been saved. And just as the Incas revered the cave of Pacaritampu, so too the others venerated the place designated for the said purpose, though not with such order nor with sacrifices so distinguished; and thus in any province one finds this type of shrine, each one shaped by its own imagination, each nation telling the story differently.

CHAPTER III

On the opinions held by these Indians regarding souls and the life after this one

On this point they held many opinions differing one from another; that in which all agreed (as far as can be determined) without any dissent, was in two substantial matters: the first, in acknowledging the immortality of the soul and that in man there is more than what the eyes can see; and the other, that the good man has glory and the wicked man punishment after this life. They demonstrated the first in the great care they took to prepare and adorn their tombs; placing within them together with the deceased all their possessions, wives, servants, and quantities of food and drink. They demonstrated the second in the teachings, exhortations, and reprimands that their priests and teachers gave to the common people, to turn them from evil and incline them toward good, at times threatening them with punishment and at other times enticing them with the reward of the next life — even though in the form that this glory and punishment is given and received they err and speak nonsense, in which it was impossible for them to be correct without the light of faith.

Some believed that, when the soul departed the body, if the person had lived well, it became a star, and that all the stars of heaven had originated thus, and that there they enjoyed glory. And if the life had been wicked, the soul went to a certain place where it suffered perpetual punishment; as to where and how this punishment was administered they also disagreed, each one imagining whatever he wished, for they had in this matter nothing fixed, settled, or binding, but rather, like people without [the] light [of faith], they went about wavering and inventing new things each day, in keeping with human weakness. Others held that souls departing bodies in some places went on to be born in other places, and that when they finally departed this life entirely — for they affirmed that the world must have an end — they would receive glory or punishment according to their merits. Other nations believed that the souls of the dead remained in this world, and that at times they were joyful and at other times afflicted, wandering vagrant and solitary, suffering hunger, thirst, cold, heat, and weariness; and that they or their phantoms [fantasmas] were wont to visit their relatives and other persons, as a sign that these were about to die or that some misfortune was about to befall them. And for this reason — believing that souls suffered hunger, thirst, and other hardships — they offered at the tombs food, drink, clothing, and other things for the benefit of the deceased; and [text breaks off]

entities_mentioned.name: Pacaritampu (cueva de Pacaritampu)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Pacariqtambo, a site in the Cusco region of Peru, traditionally identified as the cave of origin (Tampu T'oqo) of the Inca dynasty
entities_mentioned.notes: Described as the foundational ancestral shrine of the Incas, linked to both creation and flood-survival mythology. Cobo interprets the political instrumentalization of this shrine as the origin of analogous provincial shrines throughout the Inca empire.

entities_mentioned.name: guacas (guacas / huacas)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Huaca — sacred objects, places, or beings in Andean religion
entities_mentioned.notes: Cobo uses 'guacas' interchangeably with 'adoratorios' (shrines). He identifies them as provincial origin-shrines linked to flood-survival myths and ancestor-stone transformations.

entities_mentioned.name: hechiceros
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Andean ritual specialists / shamans / sorcerers
entities_mentioned.notes: Cobo identifies 'hechiceros' (sorcerers/shamans) and 'viejos' (elders) as the agents who institutionalized provincial flood myths and origin shrines in opposition to Inca dynastic claims.

results.translator_notes:
1. This segment contains no botanical, pharmaceutical, or medical preparation content; it is theological-ethnographic, covering Inca cosmology, flood myths, ancestor veneration, and eschatology. No drug/plant entity extraction applies.
2. The segment straddles two chapters: it concludes a discussion (seemingly from a prior chapter III on Pacaritampu/flood myths) and opens a new Chapter III on beliefs about the soul and afterlife. The OCR appears to have reproduced duplicate 'capítulo III' headings; this likely reflects a section and chapter numbering inconsistency in the 1653 printed source itself.
3. Several OCR corruptions were silently corrected: 'vene*raíaan' → 'veneraban'; 'gneva d€^' → 'cueva de'; '^pa^o' → 'escapado'; 'áian' → 'decían'; 'discrepallían' → 'discrepaban'; 'diserep alian' → 'discrepaban'; 'tenído' → 'tenido'; 'ciegas' resolved from 'cie^'. The final word 'po' at segment end is an unambiguous truncation; marked accordingly in the cleaned text.
4. Cobo's framing is consistently apologetic-Christian: he interprets Andean religious beliefs as diabolic deception and attributes their error to lack of Scripture and writing. This rhetorical stance is preserved faithfully in the translation.
